author: No author found
commentBody:
I'm excited to make this in the fullness of summer! I have used her cookbook, "The Gift of Southern Cooking" for many years and recommend it. (Perhaps there's a copy of it or her other books in your local library.)
Her story of growing up in an all African-Amerucan village in Freetown,Virginia is full of insight into the food culture of people who knew how to relish everything they grew, collected, hunted, or fished. Later on as an influential chef in NYC, she became one of the 
first to influence the farm to table movement
that emphasizes seasonal provisions.

author: No author found
commentBody: Just seeing Edna Lewis’ name makes me happy.  I’ve been making this cake for 30 years and shared the recipe often with friends.  Cream of Tartar is a kitchen staple for this recipe and Lewis’ famous biscuits.

author: No author found
commentBody: Please note that most commercially available double-acting baking powder does NOT have cream of tartar.  In the 1970's (because of cost?) cream of tartar was removed from single-acting baking powder and double-acting baking powder remained.  This topic was covered in the NY "Times" in a "De gustibus" column by Craig Claiborne published Jan. 5, 1976

commentBody: On the tip about making your own baking powder, that Claiborne column from 1976 has a different recipe: two parts cream of tartar, one part baking soda, one part cornstarch. Joy has a different recipe: two parts cream of tartar, one part baking soda, half part salt.

commentBody: As someone who was raised in the South, I had to smile at the thought of baking a cake while the vegetables cook at the other end of the wood stove. Most of the vegetables I ate as a child were either raw or complete mush, and the idea of cooking vegetables on the stove for 40 minutes is anathema to our more modern way of eating. Well, except for summer collard greens. I could see cooking those for 40 minutes! God bless Ms. Lewis; she was a treasure.

commentBody: does anyone have any suggestions for how to scale this for a 9*13 in pan? it sounds like a good recipe for a crowd.

author: No author found
commentBody: @Catherine Multiply the dimensions of the pan called for in the recipe (8x8=64) and the pan you want to scale up (or down) to (9x13=117). That gets you the area in square inches of each pan. Then divide the area of your pan by the area of the recipe pan: 117/64=1.828. That tells you that you’ll need to scale up the recipe by 1.8. I would probably just double it for ease of calculating amounts and then leave out a little of the batter when filling the pan if it seems like it would be too much.

author: No author found
commentBody: You could probably just double the ingredients because the 8x8 is 64 sq inches, and double that is 128 sq in. A 9x13 is 117 sq inches, so your cake will be just less than 10% too big. But it's tough to adapt recipes with eggs because "half an egg" isn't practical.

commentBody: When making the rolls, keep them as round as possible. If you flatten the rolls, the meat will be thicker at the edges. I make these regularly with various meats and they are great. Especially great with a simple homemade Turkish pide bread. Quick and easy to make. I usually crisp the meat in a hot cast iron with butter before serving.

commentBody: This one was really good and not hard, I made some tweaks as other commenters suggested. I removed the second parchment before rolling, I broiled it briefly before serving for crispiness, I also tossed the meat with lemon and some reserved drippings for more authentic greasiness. Lastly, I made a tzatziki sauce instead of just plain yogurt. I'm just a total tzatziki stan, and I compromised by replacing the oil in it with the fat from the pan.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I made half a batch using lamb only.  I still did two roll ups which was, I think, a mistake because they completely fell apart when unwrapped.  My initial bake was 30 minutes, followed by 2 minutes under the broiler.  The broiling added a nice texture and flavor.

When I tried to drain the juices halfway through, the rolls shifted and I could not completely drain the tray.  At the end, there was this burned sludge left where the juices had been.  I finished it on a fresh sheet.

I think for 1/2 batch I should have done one roll.  I think I had it too thin because it fell apart so much.  Perhaps a heavier roll would not have shifted as much during drainage.  I used purchased tzatziki so I did not use the reserved juices.

Also, I only used 1/2 or a huge red onion which was plenty.  Discretion on the onion…I think that the juicy onions may have contributed to the “sludge”.

It was still good enough to be requested again!

I used premium mixed pepper flakes and it was a bit spicier than expected!  So be careful if you don’t want it too spicy.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe is the closest I’ve found to the döner kebabs I had in Freiburg, Germany. It’s not exactly the same, but it comes pretty close. Also, they used Fladenbrot instead of pita. It’s a Turkish bread with a light, fluffy texture and a soft, slightly chewy finish. If you can’t find Fladenbrot where you live, it’s actually pretty easy to make at home.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Very good. Made it with ground turkey and it came out delicious. Definitely recommend broiling the meat after unrolling it to give a little burnish and crisp. Made a white sauce of 1/2 cup mayo, 1/2 cup greek yogurt, and about a tablespoon of tahini (it was all that was left in the jar), microplaned one large clove of garlic, drizzled in some olive oil and squeeze of lemon juice. Served with Turkish red cabbage and other veggies to wrap.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this easily in my small French kitchen with my small oven - it is significantly faster and easier than one would expect reading the instructions.  Used half lamb and half beef (and halved the recipe - I made 2 rolls).  Will definitely make again in my larger US kitchen.  I got a lot of liquid and made a sauce by adding about 1/4 cup to 1/4 c yoghurt.  I will skim the fat and continue to make my sauce "on demand" as I eat the leftovers.  (Served on a bed of roasted potato wedges.)

author: Korsha Wilson

I stuck it in the fridge uncovered, about 20 mins to tighten it up a bit.  To make sure the spices were mixed in thoroughly, I put the spices and the yogurt in the food processor after making the onion paste, and then mixed the whole mixture into the meat.  Broiled at the end as suggested by other comments. Really good and would make again.

author: Korsha Wilson

This recipe is a trip to Greece without an airplane ticket!

I didn’t removed the top sheet of parchment as others have suggested and it worked just fine. My parchment didn’t turn dark brown even after cooking it a little longer but the meat had crispy edges and was delicious.
The only think I did differently was to remove a fair bit of the fat from the pan drippings before adding it to the yogurt - oh and I added chopped mint!
Served a Greek salad in the side with feta - splendid!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: made this with impossible lite ground "beef" and it was delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I was very impressed with how delicious the lamb turned out. Perhaps a bit heavy on the red pepper flakes, I felt it dominated the other seasonings. I added chopped fresh parsley to the meat mixture. I removed the top sheet of parchment before rolling and baking and also broiled it on high for 4 minutes after unrolling the meat onto the sheet. The broiling is essential for crispy goodness. The sauce couldn't have been tastier or easier to make. I'd love to make this for my Cypriot friend!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Quite good! Cut the salt in the meat by 1/3 and it was still plenty salty and used fresh garlic in with the onion. Very solid result though and will keep this on hand as a satisfying dinner with good leftovers for lunches.

author: Korsha Wilson

I used cottage cheese instead of yogurt with the meat (all I had) and served with tahini sauce, chopped tomatoes and pickles. I only had 96% lean meat so added another tablespoon of olive oil

author: Korsha Wilson

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I found this to be strangely under seasoned. I was surprised how bland the meat was and the drippings were also lacking flavor. I will probably try again, but will definitely up the spices and salt.

It was also very soft and wet when I unrolled it after 45 minutes. I went ahead and gave it an additional 15 minutes at 500° convection which helped, but I can’t help but think it has to do with the highly varying quantity of “2 medium onions” in the ingredients. Did I have too much? Were they excessively wet? I don’t know what the recipe writer considers a medium onion (in my area, we only seem to get one size of onion), so the recipe would benefit from specifying approximate amounts in cups or grams.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I’ll start off by saying that this recipe was absolutely delicious and went by fast in my house. However, I did have a couple issues:
It took twice as long to make as the recipe says (I started cooking at 530 and dinner was on the table at 745), mainly because the spreading process is so difficult. Having the parchment sheet on top makes it so that- no matter how much meat you put -some of it will inevitably end up spilling out the sides getting all over your counter and rolling pin. In addition, if you put too much or too little meat you have no way of changing the amount without starting all over again. I found that simply using a wet hand- although a bit more tedious -ended up being less messy and gave me more flexibility for the amount of meat in my kebabs. 
I would try this again, but starting much earlier and with the different spreading technique.

So chicken is not unheard of. I personally would not use Turkey because I don't think it works with the Doner spice profile, but you can certainly try it as long as it's not breast meat.

commentBody: Questions.  Is Doner Kebab of all varieties made with this particular lamb mixture? Could this lamb mixture could be shaped onto a skewer like a sausage and then grilled to make the Doner Kebab that I had and loved back in the day when I visited Turkey?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Ruth Ann:  Döner was originally made from very thin slices of lamb, alternating with super thin slices of fat from the tail of a fat-tailed sheep. It was rotated vertically (though there is a horizontal version called cağ kebap) in front of a charcoal fire, later gas, then electric. Charcoal is way better! So this is an approximation of döner, not döner, really. Still, it is a very clever technique!

@Siobhan

The main part of this technique is about trying to reproduce the texture of the meat. Since it would be difficult to do that with Impossible brand or any meat alternative, I don’t think it would be worth all the work or use of parchment paper. 

I suggest that you use the seasonings and ingredients as a guide to the flavor profile, and just sauté or roast whatever alternative ingredient appeals to you. I’ll reduce the onion amount though, since tofu is a lighter taste than meat.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
This was a fun project and the meat did in fact end up like shaved lamb.  A few suggestions for cooks in a hurry. Remember to cut all your bottom pieces of paper first so you don’t have to tear them off with goopy hands as you go.  Also you only need to reuse one top sheet - lightly oiled so the mixture does not stick to it.  I found using a rolling pin unnecessary.  I spread the mixture first with a broad silicone spatula and my hands and then did the final spreading with the top sheet and just my hands and it turned out fine.

In terms of ingredients, I followed the recipe exactly, using all lamb and the recommended seasonings. I believed the intensity of the recommended seasonings overwhelmed the delicate lamb flavor. I would definitely use far, far less another time. Maybe use a different meat.

As for draining the fat from a heavy, shallow (foil lined) sheet pant two pounds of meat  rolling around!  Not for me!  I managed to remove it from the oven without spilling the grease all over the place but then I lifted the lamb rolls using two broad spatulas and placed them in a second foil line sheet pan for the last stage.  I let the first pan cool and the fat congeal a bit before I removed it using its foil liner.   I also think disposable foil pans are wonderful for messy situations like this.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
This recipe really appeals to me, but the directions are a little unclear for me and the ingredients are not very familiar. 

The tip about the pepper is quite helpful, but I’d never heard of guava wine. It doesn’t really seem readily available on the west coast, so I’ll use wine and guava paste. Also, once we cut the head of garlic along its equator (I assume), after it’s cooked do we squeeze out the cloves and mix it into the sauce? Or do we just let those who want the cloves take some? I guess I’m trying to figure out how garlicky this is supposed to taste. 

There also seems a lot of variation on tamarind paste. I really like tamarind flavor, but I don’t have a lot of experience cooking with it. I’m not sure how much to use of the particular jar I happen to have. 

If I make this, I will report back!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: How can you adapt this recipe to a pressure cooker?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I would use a pressure cooker instead of a Dutch oven throughout the recipe (it can double as a stovepot.) 
In Step 3,cook at pressure until the first whistle, then turn heat to low, cook for 1 hour intotal, then turn heat off, use natural release (the meat continues to cook as the pressure drops).
Continue from Step 4.
An Instant Pot combines pressure cooking with smarts - you can take a bath, read a book, or leave the house for a short errand: it will stop cooking after the set time has passed

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: If I were going to can something for long term storage I would find a recipe specifically written for that purpose. It is important that a recipe has been tested and determined safe for canning. The University of Georgia hosts the National Center for Home Food Preservation. Their site includes numerous recipes. See https://nchfp.uga.edu/how/pickle/relishes-salads/rummage-relish/

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is absolutely fabulous and most delicious!! You can play around a bit with the veggies (I added a bit more green tomatoes) but don’t change anything else. The spices/seeds, sugar and vinegar are in perfect balance. The only suggestion for canning to maintain long-term shelf life is to heat-sterilize the jars in boiling water or in the oven and immediately afterwards fill in the hot chutney. It’s the same exact process as with home-made jam, easy and safe.

commentBody: I lived in the Lower East Side in the 1980s and 1990s and once stumbled into Princess Pamela's restaurant "Princess Pamela's Southern Touch Cuisine"on East Houston with a friend one weeknight.  She looked us over and sat us down.  She yelled over to her cook (can't remember her name - maybe Euphrenia .. something) "what are we cooking tonight?"  Following came honestly one the best meals I have ever eaten. We asked if she had any liquor and she pulled out a jug of wine, and said don't tell.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Anne P. That place was epic. Nothing like it in the LES these days…

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Anne P. God I miss that era.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Why would you call for 8 tablespoons in a recipe rather than 1/2 cup??

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Because people are more likely to fit a tablespoon measure into a jam jar than they are to pour jam out into a liquid measuring cup?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: also because the measurement is likely to be more exact.  someone might eyeball 1/2 and it would be actually 3/4. if you use a tablespoon you will probably measure it accurately.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @susan can’t agree.  The total error in 8 measures will likely be greater than one measure.  I plan to fill a 1/2 cup measuring cup with a spoon small enough to fit in the jar.

author: Korsha Wilson

author: Korsha Wilson

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I've made a sauce similar to this for years. Instead of lemon juice, substitute soy sauce. Wonderful with grilled pork tenderloin and chicken.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I cannot tell how thrilled I am to try this! I actually went to Princess Pamela's (probably in the early 80's). A jazz-guitar playing friend knew about it and knew Princess Pamela's boyfriend, who was a bass player. 

It was QUITE the experience and yes, you had the feeling you might get kicked out any time (we weren't) but the food was great. If I remember correctly, Princess Pamela's mother was also there.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have fond memories of Princess Pamela and her restaurant. She was host, server, and entertainer at once. As host, as you write, she'd determine if you were welcome in. As server, she would rattle off the fixed menu of the evening then ask "now what are you having?" As entertainer, patrons would beg, "please, Princess Pamela, won't you sing for us" - she'd play shy, then give in - her cook would come out and string the bass while she serenaded the small dining room.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this and I liked how it turned out, but I think the recipe should include some measurements of salt, pepper, and Worcestershire sauce because I added a considerable amount of all three. I was born and raised in the Deep South, so any Southern food/soul food recipe that calls for black pepper is asking for a generous amount. And given all the sugar in the recipe, the salt and Worcestershire sauce are going to be used in liberal amounts to balance out the flavors.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This sauce is absolutely delicious. It went perfectly with salmon, and I can't wait to make it with fried chicken.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: More Princess Pamela recipes please. I’ve lost my cookbook. Still live around the corner from her 10th St restaurant, what a rickety building that was but the food was fabulous. Pamela’s and the Pink Teacup in the West Village were my introduction to soul food.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Hope fig jam works too!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I was a regular at both places she had. The first was over a Thai restaurant (maybe 10th Street), whose owners she called "the Formosians," mistaking Thailand for Taiwan.  She was quite a character, and would often sit at our table and regale us with stories. The second was the one on Houston Street. It was usually two choices, and the sides were the same whichever you chose. Great food, and fond memories of the waitress she had, who would shuffle around in a starched 1960s uniform and wig.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I remember eating Princess Pamela’s smothered pork chops when I lived in the east village. A rare treat!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Thank you!  I have a 1969 copy of Princess Pamela's Soul Food Cookbook. I just now noticed that it is autographed.   The cover is missing, the back page is torn off and the pages are yellowed. I  gave away 50 cookbooks and kept 5.  This one because of the scribble scrabble on the shrimp boil page, the memories and the Milk Baked Ham recipe.   Thank you!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Glorious memories of Princess Pamela’s, but can’t say the food was at the top of the list! Oh but the experience was not to be beat.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @TPJ You didn’t rate the food top of the list because you thought there was better soul food to be had, or because it wasn’t a type of cuisine you particularly enjoyed? Genuinely curious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Was excited to try this on a pork tenderloin: VERY sweet. Dialed that down with a splash of soy but wished I had ignored the “add a half cup of water” note. There’s oil in here and you can see the separation, plus it’s as thin as consommés. I’d hold off and add water as needed.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can Worcestershire be omitted? I have never been able to acquire the taste for it. Are there any substitutions that could work?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Sherry - You ask if Worcestershire sauce can be omitted.  Sure!  The recipe says "to taste" for amount, so leaving it out works fine.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I made this and it was tasty, but very thin. Following directions to add 1/2 cup water and then I let it boil/simmer forever.  It never thickened.
I guess I would make again, but maybe not add the full amount of water, or even at all.

author: Korsha Wilson

It was a rather strange spot, in which her "guests" were locked in after gaining admittance, and whose food was...err...secondary to the experience. It was all a bit, well eccentric is a kind word, but scary is perhaps more accurate.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Vis-a-vis jam: the easiest thing is to look at the jam label.  See how much a serving (1 tbsp) weighs, then weigh that x8 right into your saucepan.  In fact, weighing any ingredients right into the recipe saves washing measuring cups, spoons, etc.  More accurate, too.  
BTW, a quickie sauce with a very similar result.  Equal parts jam and prepared mustard heated with a little butter right into the microwave makes a delicious sauce for baked chicken, pork chops, fish, etc. etc. in about 2 minutes

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was watery and never thickened despite boiling a very long time. I used Bon Maman peach preserves. I’m thinking the jam used in the original recipe was a thicker preserve. Maybe Smuckers?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Eight tablespoons of most fruit jams are usually @120 g by Weight.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: In the 80’s we had Miss Anne’s in Fort Greene on South Oxford, just at the top of the Lafayette C subway station steps. It was a lower level take out in a house … no choices just what she made that day. I don’t know if it was as good as PP’s but we thought it was the real deal.

Seven tablespoons seemed like a lot for a pound of meat. author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made mine gluten free. I used a gluten free baguette--DON'T DO IT. The coconut milk made it mush.  HOWEVER, the meatballs came out fine with the white mush added. I rolled in brown rice flour and that absorbed some wetness. Reduced the epis in the meatballs to 5 T, per other suggestions that they were too spicy. The spice level was fine, as they still had bite and the sauce was spicy, too. I also cooked the sauce down about 30 minutes after adding the onions and they were still crisp.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: A few modifications this time: gluten free breadcrumbs in the meatballs, browning them in only 1-2 T olive oil and keeping them warm while I make the sauce. Because the sauce is meant to be watery, I cook it down for at least 30 minutes, adding the meatballs to cook through and the sliced onions. Threw in handfuls of spinach on the side and cooked for a just a minute or so. Was excellent served with basmati rice and sauce over everything. And the epis is great to use in other cooking.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Collards are a great candidate for an instant pot, and there's no reason not to include the stems. I separate and dice the stems and give them a head start by sauteing them with the onions. Add the liquid and simmer for a few minutes (part of the head start). Then add the greens and wilt, pop on the lid, and cook under pressure for about 10 minutes. This also gets more flavor out of the turkey or ham, especially if you include the bones.

commentBody: @Andante cooking noodles releases starches into the water. Cooking them in the broth will affect the look and possibly texture of the soup. Also, if you don’t plan to eat the soup all at once, leaving the noodles in the broth will affect their texture. If none of this is an issue for you, go ahead!

author: Korsha Wilson

author: Korsha Wilson

author: Korsha Wilson

Other good substitutes would be ham hock, smoked turkey necks, or a whole turkey drumstick - and I think chicken would be fine in place of turkey.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious! I couldn't find a smoked turkey wing, so I used a smoked turkey drum. I used the Instant Pot, and the whole thing came together in about an hour. author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My Tennessee grandmother cooked greens for dinner at least once a week, and we grandkids waited around the stove until they were done, when she would portion out little glasses of potlikker for us to drink.  Does this recipe work with thick-sliced bacon, pork belly, or ham hock instead of smoked turkey wing?  Does it work with kale, mustard greens, Swiss chard, or beet greens?  I'd love to try a variation that would remind me of my grandmother's no-recipe-just-what-feels-right version.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
In case anyone else needs this information.

Benedictine and Green Chartreuse are both herbal French liqueurs, with Benedictine having a slightly sweeter, more balanced flavor profile, while Green Chartreuse is known for its strong, herbaceous, and slightly peppery taste due to its higher alcohol content and more potent herbal blend; both are traditionally produced by monks in their respective monasteries. 

I would definitely go with the sweeter one.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I didn't know: Putting the butter in the freezer helps ensure it remains very cold until added to the sauce at the end of cooking. When cold butter is incorporated into a hot sauce slowly and gradually, it emulsifies better, creating a smoother, silkier sauce with a velvety texture. If the butter is too warm or melted when added, it can separate, making the sauce greasy instead of creamy. Freezing the butter briefly ensures optimal emulsification, resulting in a rich, glossy finish to your sauce

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I cut the tabasco to make it edible for my wife, served it over cavatelli, and it was a hit with the family. I doubled the reciped and probably used about 40 percent of the Tabasco called for (slightly under one tablespoon instead of two full ones). I'm a big fan of heat and it had a satisfying fire wthout feeling the need to add any additional Tabasco, so those with a sensitive food palate might want to go easy on the Tabasco, at least on their first try.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: We loved this dish to pieces, but it is quite rich. Thinking it would be too spicy, I cut the Tabasco in half and finished the sauce, but then added the rest of the Tabasco after tasting. The sauce was sufficient for another half-pound of shrimp. Next time we may try cutting the butter to two Tbsp. and/or using half and half instead of cream. Also, the cooking times are too long for tender shrimp, so I advise less time for the initial sauté and a quick warm-up at the end. Crusty sourdough was perfect for sopping, but we might serve over steamed greens next time.

@Mark in Lacey good tips! I loved this but would love to make modifications so I could have it on a heavier rotation! Was also trying to think of alternatives to the cream and I think with half the butter it would still be great. 

Oh yes - I made this exactly per the recipe and loved it!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Has anyone made this as called for in the recipe? I’m interested in your reaction to it as the thought of a TABLESPOON of Tabasco in a dish with pound of shrimp and sauce sounds a bit like a death wish.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Doug C. It’s hard to know how to respond to this because it wasn’t too bad for me…but then again I have friends who can’t take any heat and could never have eaten this. I can’t eat the very hot salsa that’s for sale in the grocery store where I live in Texas…this was definitely hot but not inedibly so for me. Actually I think the heat was the pepper as much as the Tabasco. It was fab though we were licking the plate.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Doug C. I’ve not made it….yet, plan to & plan to use the full amount of tobacco b/c I surmise (from experience) that the tabasco‘s heat will end up cut considerably by the heavy cream. granted I LOVE hot food :)

author: Korsha Wilson
Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this today for lunch. It is my kind of recipe- flavorful, quick and easy, and made of ingredients I had in the pantry. I was able to cook all of the shrimp in 1 tablespoon of olive oil in the original browning phase. I also used sriracha - about 3/4 tablespoon - because that is what I had in the house. Black pepper is pleasingly prominent. Served over a baked potato. Will definitely make again!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used dry vermouth for the wine, whole milk yogurt for the cream, half a white onion for shallot. Could have used more shrimp. I had an Italian hot sauce with Calabria chilies and only used 1 tbsp of worstie. finished with Parm regg. Yum.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Thank you for the completely different recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @J Pls tell me @ yogurt instead of cream. When did you add? Did it separate? I’m averse to so much cream. Thx

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Used about 3/4 tbsp Tabasco and it wasn’t too spicy.  And used chunks of chicken breast instead of shrimp.  Will make it this way again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I can’t wait to try this sauce with chicken or pork and some linguine! It’s possibilities are endless…@c.boyardee

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made this with Franks Red Hot Sauce and found it had the perfect amount of heat. Used 1.17 lb shrimp and 10 oz of scallop without adjusting other ingredients and found it sauce-y enough since the extra protein also brought moisture. Served with toasted sourdough, roasted green beans and broccolini. Looking forward to repeating this one.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: We really liked it and added more Tabasco and crushed red peppers. I added more cream than called for as hubby loves sopping up sauce with bread. Definitely would be great for a crowd if you doubled it. All you need is some bread and a salad.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used 1/2 the hot sauce called for and although I didn’t think the full measure would’ve been too hot, it delightfully allowed the black pepper to come through a little bit more prominently. I served it over pasta. Make sure you’ve got your mise en place as the wonderful sauce and this dish comes together quite quickly.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I made this exactly as stated and it was pretty much perfect. A hair rich for my taste, but a lovely treat. Eat a salad in advance of this so that you aren’t quite as tempted to drink the sauce.

We ate this over roasted potatoes and it was divine, but I suspect anything would be delicious with this.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Meant to say A Hit.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Excellent and delicious. Trader Joes has a frozen "seafood blend" which includes shrimp, calamari and scallops which worked beautifully. Didn't have a shallot on hand so subbed half a diced yellow onion. Used 1/4 t dried rosemary and 1/2 t dried thyme (I'm sure the fresh would be better but . . . life.)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe is going in my repeat box! We did cut the Tabasco in half because we have spice averse people and it was delicious even for them. The cream cuts the spice a ton. Served it with crusty bread but over mashed potatoes or pasta would be great too.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Added a handful of frozen spinach leftover from something else. It went in at the same time as the hot sauce.  Added a pretty green color and herbaceous quality.  We really loved this and will make it again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: A really yummy shrimp dish! I served it with brown rice, which was the perfect complement to the creamy sauce. I didn't have Worcestershire sauce (forgot I ran out), so just left it out, and I imagine it would only improve this very delicious dish. The only thing I would do differently is make more sauce!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
In Texas and Louisiana we use a full tablespoon of Tabasco.
New Englanders, you might want to use a dash. 
I learned the hard way, do not use 1/2 and 1/2 if you don’t have heavy cream, it’s too light and will separate. 
Another good but overly complicated NY Times recipe.  Cutting butter into chunks and freezing it?  Really?  Let’s eat!
The overall concept is good, just run with it and improvise. 
Served over rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I was just wondering about using half and half. Will now go to the store for heavy cream.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Really easy and yummy!! We are a high spice household so I ended up doubling the Tabasco and pepper.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I find it simpler to cook the shrimp in the sauce at the end.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Hi Judith, I have not made this yet but  am leery of recipes that call for cooking shrimp until almost done, removing them, making a sauce, then adding the shrimp back into the pan. 
 Seems to me to be a recipe for overcooked shrimp. Did you add the shrimp right before you added the butter (which is what I was thinking about doing as I read the recipe) or afterwards?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this exactly as written.  Such an amazing, delicious and easy recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Great flavors BUT don't need to cook the shrimp first (as they got a bit rubbery); next time we will cook the shrimp in the sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was outstanding! I did not have a shallot and used 1/4 of a sweet onion. I also used dried rosemary and thyme. Doubled Tabasco as well. Served with some crusty garlic bread and I will be making this again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Putting the butter in the freezer helps ensure it remains very cold until added to the sauce at the end of cooking. When cold butter is incorporated into a hot sauce slowly and gradually, it emulsifies better, creating a smoother, silkier sauce with a velvety texture. If the butter is too warm or melted when added, it can separate, making the sauce greasy instead of creamy. Freezing the butter briefly ensures optimal emulsification, resulting in a rich, glossy finish to your sauce

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Oh, my.

As we were eating this, my husband looked at me with gravitas and said, “Please save this to the recipe file.”

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: It was great and easy! I did not have Tabasco so i used Franks hot sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Admittedly I didn’t have several of the sauce ingredients so had to improvise, eg gin instead of white wine. However, I thought the thick creamy sauce overpowered the shrimp which has a delicate flavour. I think it would be ok on steak.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Cut back on the butter and cream as others suggested. Next time I’ll only u

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Soooo yummy with a hot, crunchy loaf of bread.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: While we were eating, Kevin couldn’t stop yelling about how good this is.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delish. Great new take on shrimp and rice. I replaced the heavy cream with coconut cream.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Excellent. Used only one mini bottle of Tabasco. Husband thought spice level was perfect. Used water and a little additional lemon juice instead of wine.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was delicious with just slight modifications. I didn’t have fresh herbs so used 1/2 tsp of both rosemary and thyme. Also, I used half and half instead of cream (what I had on hand). I added the 2 TBSP of butter at the end and it didn’t need another 2 to thicken it. The 1 TBSP hot sauce was enough for my husband but I added more. We ate it on pasta and it really was delicious. Next time I will add cooked asparagus or other green vegetable to help sop up the sauce and instead of pasta try a really good crusty bread. So many possibilities!  Thanks for this recipe!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This did NOT work for us. Made as written, and the sauce basically just tasted like Worcestershire sauce. I did like the cook on the shrimp- they were perfect. But next time I’ll just improv a garlic butter sauce…this recipe just wasn’t appealing enough to try again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This. Dish. SLAPS! It’s so flavorful, so easy, so complex in flavor… I like it with gnocchi.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe will be in my regular rotation!! My husband doesn’t do dairy so I subbed plant butter sticks and cream of coconut milk (coco lopez) to make it non-dairy. Deliciousness!! You could also use full fat coconut milk - just chill the can in fridge ahead of time so the fat is at the top. I used the full tbsp of Frank’s hot sauce and that combo with the coconut lopez was perfect for me. I like a hint of spice that lingers as you eat.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this with the full Tablespoon of Tabasco, and it had a slight kick, but I didn’t think it was too hot at all! Very tasty!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made exactly as the recipe instructs, and my son and I think it’s perfect.  We ladle the sauce over blanched green beans, the shrimp, and dip everything up with a baguette.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Why does the butter go in the freezer?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: So the sauce doesn’t break (separate) as easily.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I believe it's so that it will mostly emulsify in the sauce, thickening it slowly, rather than just adding it for taste. I've seen this technique before.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I believe freezing the butter makes it easier tp emulsify it smoothly into the sauce.  Freezing the butter allows it to slowly mix in with the rest of the sauce.  Using cold butter and adding it in small amounts at a time is another method often used.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used Nando‘s hot sauce, otherwise followed the recipe. It was s hit.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Used water, a half teaspoon of sugar and a little additional lemon instead of wine. Used half the amount of hot sauce and heat level was good for my spouse.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: OK, Texan here - just adding Tabasco sauce does not BBQ it make! (Said like Moira from Schitt’s Creek) Use a tablespoon of real spicy BBQ sauce (or more) and it is delicious! I tried with beer too instead of wine and also not bad. Overall really like this recipe because love shrimp!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Janet W It’s not BBQ in the traditional sense. The New Orleans style BBQ shrimp that this recipe is inspired by is shrimp cooked with butter, Worcestershire sauce, and peppers, both black and red. I don’t know why it’s named that but it’s a very traditional Creole recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Definitely do not follow the recipe quantity for the hot sauce--it was inedible w/the spice level.  Overall--a rather unimaginative flavor provile--the hot sauce and pepper overwhelms everything else--also not sure what makes this "BBQ."  Do not recommend.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This based on a classic dish from NoLa that is called BBQ shrimp. It has all of the major elements and is served across the city.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Not sure what I did wrong but I ended up with way too much sauce, it was too heavy with the cream and I would use a quarter of the Worcestershire sauce. There was nothing BBQ y about it but I defense of the recipes I think the author was only alluding to BBQ. Not going to try this again sadly. On to the next new recipe, however.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Victoria A It’s not traditional BBQ they’re referring to—the dish is based on New Orleans style BBQ shrimp which is not like BBQ very much at all— it’s shrimp cooked with a lot of butter, Worcestershire sauce, and pepper.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Used 1 tsp Tabasco and that was plenty. Unless you like fire, start with less and then you can always add more.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made this last week and it's outstanding. This is going into the rotation.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was excellent. I served it over noodles with the shrimp and sauce. I skipped the Tabasco and added a few drops of franks hot sauce. The frozen butter is a great addition I would not have thought of to use. Worcestershire was an odd ingredient to me but it worked well. I will definitely make this again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. I did cut back on the Tabasco because I don’t handle spice well, but did enough to give it a little kick. Served it with some homemade crusty bread. Will definitely be making this again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: We thought we could have used more of the sauce.  It was enough for 2 but not 4.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The spice level in this recipe is great and well-balanced. A tbsp of hot sauce may sound like a lot, but it's competing with 1/2c of cream and 1/2 stick of butter. I hedged my bets and did 50/50 Tabasco and Cholula, which was delicious. May try others in the future!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Cooked this on January 11, '25. Loved it! I used Sriracha for the hot sauce, great taste.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Followed the recipe pretty exactly but only had 3/4 pound of shrimp for 2 people. (A pound for four seems a bit meager?) Did cut the butter in half and the sauce was plenty rich.  Served over Basmati rice. Could use a sprinkle of chopped Italian parsley or other fresh herb on top but fine without it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Use Frank's or Louisana - anything but Tabasco and heat profile goes down alot .......not to mention taste better.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
The Lobster Pot in P-Town. My first job after my freshman year at Boston University aka BU.
When Dick LaBrie owned it, Before Chef Tim McNulty and this fabulous recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe is delicious. It is a very truncated version of (imho) the standard for all NOLA bbq shrimp, Emeril’s. (That recipe can be found in his first cookbook.) It is a bit light on the bay laurel/creole flavor of Emeril’s, but it is still Worcestershire-forward enough to be authentic.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The butter is frozen so it will melt slowly and fully incorporate into the sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe, served it over cavatappi and was very happy with the results. I would like to propose a new name: Astonishingly Delicious Creamy Spicy Shrimp. That should take care of any barbecue confusion.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is absolutely divine and don’t change a thing. Crusty warm baguette is all you need on the side (along with Sauvignon Blanc)!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
This is a wonderful recipe. As a first-timer, I followed it exactly. I noted that it called for one tablespoon of hot sauce, not two and two tablespoons of Worcestershire sauce. This gave it a very pleasant, mild heat.
A word to the wise - this comes together very quickly once the shrimp are browned so you absolutely must have your mise en place ready to go. Then it’s really easy! Lovely dinner!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made exactly as written...easy and excellent. Served with polenta cakes. A keeper!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My cream had gone bad so used 1/2 and 1/2 and it came out just fine.  Oh, also, I forgot to add the black pepper which I think will take it to next level but everyone thought it perfect.  I did 1.5 of the recipe and it was scant for 4 people, recommend doubling for a party of 4.  YUM!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe is a WINNER! No reason to cook the shrimp first. I added just after the cream and before the butter. Absolutely delicious! (I knew I had to try it when I saw it was from the Lobster Pot!)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The shrimp is WAAAY better with a sear on it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Not for the fat phobic but definitely worthy of the five star rating. Served with pasta but I think rice might work even better.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I had to modify quite a bit to work with what I had on hand. I had 3 lbs shrimp but not enough ingredients to 3x the recipe so I doubled the sauce (still plenty of sauce). I used shiner beer instead of wine, onion instead of shallot. Only 1 tbsp Tabasco. I added black pepper to taste (lots of it, but not 3 tsp). Dry herbs in place of fresh. Used cajun seasoning on the shrimp which added extra heat. Served with sourdough bread. Husband said it was a top 3 dinner. It's going in the permanent file.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made this today, was one of the tastiest sauces I’ve ever had. Used a full tablespoon of Franks Red Hot, because that’s what I had, and it was perfect (but I like spicy).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have made this dish twice now and I really do enjoy it. Few dishes use cooking the shrimp to impart the shrimp flavor into the dish for depth of flavor like this one does. Adding a glug more of cream to the sauce (if you need to) anytime after it starts cooking will not hurt the sauce at all. This is really nice served over polenta.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I followed the recipe and the results were DELICIOUS. A dish to be made again and gain.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I’ve pretty much cut dairy fats out of my diet per Dr. order, so heavy cream is not really an option for me, though I could use half of the butter called for. I’m wondering about using full fat coconut milk instead of the cream. I realize this will completely change the flavor of the recipe, but it seems to me it would still be delicious. Thoughts?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Mark I subbed coco lopez and used plant base butter and it was still delicious. Full fat coconut would work, too, and likely not be as sweet as the coco lopez but I found the combo of the sweetness with the spice was great for me.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Nikki in DC  Thanks Nikki!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Very tasty and easily adapted to suit ingredients on hand. Served it with rice. I'll make it again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I usually tweak recipes - this one is perfect and fantastic as written. The amount of tabasco, Worcestershire and ground pepper works perfectly. I use a bit less pepper if I don't have the coarse ground version. I find the heat in the low-medium zone (the cream and butter tone it way down) and for our family I tend to add more tabasco/Frank's. Excellent sauce even for chicken. We serve with bread to mop up the sauce or over pasta. I usually triple the recipe for our big clan and it is a hit!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was delicious! I used a pound of shrimp and a pound of scallops, doubled the sauce ingredients but did 1 3/4 the amount of butter. It fed four of us perfectly. I guess the appetites are larger in our house.  Also I just added the shrimp and scallops to the sauce per the other comments (no cooking ahead of the sauce) and cooked on super low for maybe 5 minutes covered, stirring occasionally. Was definitely spicy but we like spice. Race reviews from the group, even my husband who isn’t wild about shrimp.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The sauce was incredible, but only when paired with the shrimp and bread. It is a punch in the face on its own but a flavor explosion while accompanied.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Why do recipes insist on leaving the tails on shrimp? Is the visual worth the extra work for the diner?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@Tucker
Tails add flavor to the sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: What if I left out the heavy cream?  Trying to cut calories and I'm a lactode!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Joan E. I’ve had success with coconut milk or canned coconut cream in other recipes. I’m going to give that a try with this one.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Where is the BBQ part?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Cory Remjeske The “BBQ” part is just an old New Orleans naming convention.The original creator of the dish thought the sauced shrimp looked like barbecue, hence the name. To be fair, most NOLA interpretations are more Worcestershire-intense, and the shells are left on, so it kinda made sense.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I could add this comment to a million recipes; my wife is allergic to garlic. Is there anything I can use as a substitute?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I know nothing about garlic allergy so this might be silly, but is she also allergic to garlic powder?  You might try minced fresh ginger instead.  Completely different flavor than garlic, but might also be delicious, and adds a similar slight "sting" on the palate.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I can't eat garlic either and use garlic infused olive oil. The part I am sensitive to is not fat soluble.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I really dislike Tabasco, or any sauce with a lot of vinegar, so I tripled the amount of black pepper,  and it came out great.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Why freeze the butter?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Any ideas of what to sub for the cream if you're lactose intollerant?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@RP
Coconut cream is my substitute of choice. I am lactose intolerant too. Alternatively Cocojune coconut yogurt  or Wegmans brand coconut yogurt.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: A tablespoon of tabasco might be too much.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Basically a riff on shrimp etoufee.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Where is the BBQ in this?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Why is it called BBQ shrimps? What’s BBQ about this recipe?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
The frozen butter chunks melt more slowly and blend into the sauce well
 Others have explained it bett r r in other comments.  @Claire Duncan

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used chili crisp ..... YUM!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
The dish was great, got the highest rating ever from my wife. 
However, cooking times for shrimp were WAY too long. Recipe called for cooking the shrimp 2-3 minutes per side. I cooked less than a minute per side and the shrimp in the finished dish bordered on overcooked.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: It was perfect! My only suggestion is: I assumed that the way they had you cook the shrimp, that they should only be mostly cooked. But they didn't quite cook through when added to the skillet on low. So cook them until they are just cooked through, or when you put the shrimp back in, turn the heat back up to medium to let them finish.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This came across on my NYT recipe sub today and decided to make this for dinner instead of ordering Friday night pizza…boy…sure glad I did. It was excellent. Only used about 9 very large/jumbo shrimp for 2 but kept the sauce quantities the same. Used Chipotle Tabasco and just under a tablespoon. The sauce flavors are outstanding. Served with warmed slices of Kalamata and Green Olive bread and Green Salad with Sour Cream and Onion Dressing. We were discussing how left over shrimp on a slice of bread, sauce over and melted cheese might mingle…hmmm.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made it exactly as written and it was excellent.  Too fatty for a regular night at home, but perfect for company when you want something fairly easy and quick.  Served with crusty bread and hubby would have like pasta.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Thanks for this excellent, elegant recipe. We’ve passed by the Lobster Pot numerous times over the years but never ate there. Now we’ve eaten there vicariously!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used a tbsp of  Crystal hot sauce instead of Tabasco because I was out. Crystal is still a cayenne sauce - but not as hot. It was perfect. I did everything else per the recipe.  I will make it again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made This Dish, Tonite, Pretty Much as Written (although I Did Cut the Tabasco sauce to 1/2 tbs), I Was Only Preparing 1/2 lb. of Shrimp, And My Tolerance For Spicy/Hot is on the Lower End. The Sauce is on the Velvety, Rich Side, But Complex w/  Layers of Flavor. Both the Cream and the Butter at the End Absolutely Quiet Down the Heat. Definitely Reminded Me of a New Orleans/Creole Dish You'd Find in the French Quarter. As One Reader Noted, Important to Have Everything in Place before you Begin.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Use Cholula instead of Tobasco. Less than 1Tbsp also.  Cholula has flavor as well as enough heat. I am a former big Tobasco fan, my daughter used to say, “Dad, you would use Tobasco on ice cream!”. I like Cholula much better now because flavor is key.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious. I followed recipe except for the hot sauce. I did not use any during the cooking but added Crystal hot sauce to my plated portion. My wife prefers a lower spice level so she was happy with just the level provided by the black pepper. Thanks Sam Siftion for mentioning this recipe in your 3/21 newsletter.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Bold flavors made sublime by all the delicious dairy!  Really good over polenta spiked with Parmesan.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Worked well for 2. Sauce was fab. Used sriracha, because it’s what we had, but only half the quantity. Didn’t change anything else. Served on garlic & olive oil quinoa, but next time I think I’ll try fettuccine. The sauce has a consistency so much like a good Alfredo.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Karen L Davis we used orrechiette and it was perfect!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious. Used more than a tbsp of hot sauce because I need a little kick with my food. Fam was confused with the name - it's neither BBQ-y nor Peppery.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I kind of misread how much prep work this would be for the Monday after spring break lol. It's a bit of prep. I have a culinary degree and have cooked professionally, and this took me an hour start to finish. That being said, this is DELICIOUS! Honestly, a TB of Crystal (the hot sauce I use) wasn't even that hot after all that cream and butter gets added in. We even added extra hot sauce at the table. 

This is a keeper. Make as written, even if you're scared of the hot sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@Amy
Crystal Hot Sauce - now and forever!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Unfortunately the NYT seems to think that all subscribers have kitchen prep help on speed dial.  I've just completed the prep work.  Peeling shrimp, prepping shallot and garlic, grinding pepper took well over half an hour alone.  Looking forward to trying it since I have a sibling who lives in P-town part time, and raves about the restaurant.  But have given up hoping for a more realistic approach in terms of prep time (and ingredients.  What is a medium shallot, when chopped?).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: old bay is the best sub. don't forget to undercook the shrimp in the first step

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
This is a really tasty dish and I am glad that I read all the comments as we are not fans of heat. My modifications are:
Used sriracha instead of Tabasco- half the amount 
Added 2 tbsp of BBQ sauce- next time will use 1 tablespoon 
Used half the amount of butter
Half and half instead of heavy cream 
Halved the black pepper 
Skipped the rosemary and thyme and instead used fresh basil to finish the plate. 
Definitely adding this to my repertoire.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Rich, but delish!  I am lactose intolerant so I used Ripple half-and-half instead of heavy cream. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly.  Served with crostini.  We both loved it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I forgot to add the butter completely and this was still SO GOOD

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: We made this recipe with about 1 1/2 pounds of shrimp (21-25 tail on), no other changes and served it over thin spaghetti and/or crusty baguette. It’s going in the rotation. Delicious!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
This is great stuff, but a couple of suggestions:

1. Rather than sprigs of rosemary and thyme, use ¼ of a teaspoon of each.

2. Rather tan pasta or bread, serve over plain white rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was delicious. An absolute keeper. Spicy and creamy and tangy. Served over polenta for a twist on shrimp and grits.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Halved the Tabasco and used only tsp pepper.  Delicious over brown rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Spectacular!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I substituted Cholula for the Tabasco. I have two young children. Cholula is less piquant and has garlic. It was a hit with the kids.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Any thoughts on using full fat coconut milk as a substitute for the heavy cream? We are dairy-free and this looks delish!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: See note from Nikki 2 below.  She used cream of coconut.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was delicious. Definitely going into our rotation. We will do 1.5x or 2x as this was closer to two servings than four for us.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly except maybe an extra Tbs or two of cream. Came together quickly once garlic/shallot chopped. Wished I'd browned the shrimp a touch longer. Serves about 2 1/2. Yum!.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: question on the thyme and rosemary sprigs.  should you just strip and chop the leaves off the sprigs? Or just throw them in with the wine?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Just throw them in, then pick the stems out later. So much easier! Cheers!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Sorry to ask this if it's already been asked, but can anyone recommend a non-dairy substitute for the heavy cream? Don't get me wrong, I LOVE dairy products but my stomach doesn't agree, so any hints, tips, or cheats would be much appreciated.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Way too much Tabasco. Use half and it should be good.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I am (generally) a terrible and certainly uninventive cook (I even use that term generously ;) ).  I am, however, good at following directions.  This was truly delicious, and my wife and daughter ate large portions!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was delicious. I don’t think it tastes like BBQ but tastes like scampi with a little spice. It’s so tasty I would try to make this sauce with other meats or mushrooms.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Tobasco-style hot sauce varies considerably by brand in heat and salt level. I would taste the hot sauce to assess heat-level and the label for sodium-level (In the grocery, some time, take a look at sodium levels on the various hot sauces). Correct seasoning at the end, after incorporating the butter (I like salted), by adding more Tabasco if desired, then salt, if needed.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The protein/sauce ratio should vary according to the side. Pasta, normally (that would not be me!) not a lot. Bread, rice, or roast potatoes will want more.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious.  I subbed chipotle tabasco for regular tabasco for a bit of smokiness and reduced the black pepper a bit (spice averse partner), and the sauce was to die for.  Served over linguini and decided the best restaurant is my own kitchen.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made exactly to recipe and it was a hit! Only thing my husband asked to change is cut the Worcestershire sauce by half.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious - my husband called it "restaurant quality". Made it exactly as suggested. Used jumbo shrimp so the 2 1/2 minutes per side was fine, but if the shrimp were smaller than jumbo, I'd recommend less searing time. The tabasco and pepper did not make it too spicy. Served it with crusty bread.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Put the grilled bread on the bottom and soak it with shrimp and sauce! This is amazing and delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: You have to use heavy cream and not half and half or it will separate with any acid, as I learned with a different NYT recipe calling for heavy cream. Next time I’ll know. (That recipe used lemon juice in the sauce, this has wine and not sure what reducing the wine will mean to the cream.)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious, but the flavor only came together after adding back the partially cooked shrimp (and those juices). Sauce is so good I could drink it right from the pan!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: fabulous shrimp.   not quite pascals manles bbq shrimp, but a delicious variation on that theme.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Sorry, but that amount of Tabasco makes this recipe nearly inedible. First, I would find the original McNulty recipe if you want a really tasty dinner BUT also use you judgement with regards to the amount of ground black pepper  - it calls for 5TBS while 2 might be a better starting point. You will also see that the original recipe calls for only 1/2 tsp of Tabasco compared to 1TBS in the NYT recipe. Both recipes have the same ingredients but the original is a much better balance.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Way too much tabasco which skews the balance of flavors. The original McNulty Lobster Pot recipe is far superior.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I was so disappointed with this recipe. Followed to a T and just hated it. I have enjoyed so many other recipes from NYT and this was just a fail. Texas gal that loves spice and the Tabasco and Wooster sauce amounts were completely overwhelming. Always fun to try something new in the kitchen, but this is a one and done for me. My husband said he liked it, but he was starting to appease my mood LOL

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was fantastic! I was concerned that it called for so much Tabasco and black pepper, but it was perfect. If you like really hot stuff you could add even more!Served it with linguini.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: SO delicious! Spicy, creamy, savory, used salted butter and didn’t need to add salt aside from a lite dusting on the shrimp before cooking. Made it exactly as described plus a bit more shrimp and broccoli and broccolini as sides, plus a loaf of rustic Italian bread. Next time if adding more shrimp would also have a bit more sauce, otherwise perfect!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This sauce is BOMB. Used Frank's Red Hot. Delicious perfection

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: High return for relatively little effort! I did use Greek yogurt instead of cream, red onion instead of shallot because that’s what I had and vermouth was not shy about the Tabasco. It was excellent. I put it with good ravioli.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Delicious. I made it once with just shrimp tails and a second time with whole shrimp (with head). If you are able to, I recommend using whole shrimp. The liquid gold (shrimp brains) just brings the entire dish together, adding a sweet accent to the tangy, creamy, spicy sauce.

I didn't have fresh thyme or rosemary, I used dried and it still tasted fantastic!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have eaten kilt lettuce all my life but never from old lettuce.  Living in the North Carolina mountains we highly anticipated those first leaves of fresh lettuce in the spring.  We cut up spring onions with the lettuce and killed it with bacon grease or more likely fatback grease but never with store bought iceberg lettuce.  Sometimes we could find wild branch lettuce which worked very well.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Bonnie Dodson Exactly. I took one look at the picture and could tell it was all wrong.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Kilt lettuce is made from fresh lettuce, straight from the garden.  It is wilted by the hot bacon grease.  Not from old wilted lettuce.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Romaine? Should be leaf lettuce straight from the garden. Wilted lettuce salad - first taste of spring in East Tennessee. Or killed salad, kilt is what bagpipers wear! Anyhow, always a favorite.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My grandmother from Tennessee did not use honey nor old lettuce (yuck).  She used red leaf lettuce and scallions, mixed the greens and bacon together. Then she made sure the bacon grease was almost smoking hot before pouring it over the mix. Then added the vinegar and salt to taste.  She called it wilted lettuce. One of my favorite dishes as a child.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I’ve never made this but have eaten it, and it’s delicious. Some cooks add chopped or sliced hard-boiled eggs to it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The word killed,or kilt,has nothing to do with the state of the lettuce.  It is made with the first spring greens, or early leaf lettuce, which is “killed” or wilted, by the hot drippings. Iceberg lettuce is a contrivance which never has had, nor will have, a role in this ancient Appalachian dish.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I’ll add my vote to the consensus here that this dish, which my north Louisiana nana called wilted lettuce salad, is made from fresh leaf lettuce, never ever iceberg, fresh or otherwise. Green onions, hot bacon grease, apple cider vinegar and chopped boiled eggs are essential.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I've never heard of kilt lettuce salad being made with old lettuce, Lordy me! It's a spring delicacy made with fresh-picked greens (usually wild and/or leaf lettuces; nobody's growing iceberg lettuce in the mountains). Many generations of my Appalachian Kentucky family relish kilt lettuce as the first fresh salad of spring, with thinly sliced fresh radishes and green onions or wild ramps.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Use cabbage rather than lettuce and you  get Hot German slaw that this Yankee boy grew up eating.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This brings me back to my childhood. Agreed with others though who use fresh lettuce, not wilted, for this dish. My favorite version uses about half the vinegar and no sweetener.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Killed, or kilt, lettuce is generally considered a spring dish, once commonly made with wild branch lettuce, but it can be made with any fresh, tender-crisp salad greens—not lettuce that has been languishing on the counter or in the fridge.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Grew up with kilt lettuce. Used leaf lettuce from the garden. east tennessee classic.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
It’s October near the Great Lakes and I had all the ingredients in my kitchen, but the aging iceberg lettuce just looked too pathetic. Outside in my garden I still had some Green Wave mustard greens growing. Bushels of it!  It has more delicate leaves than some other leafy greens, but is still mustard. Raw, it is very spicy, but cooked it is mild and sweet. It vaguely resembles a lettuce, but spicy enough raw that the rabbits won’t eat it.

 I substituted a generous amount of the Green Wave mustard greens and used it instead of the tired iceberg.  I tore the leaves with my hands and included the ribs and stems.  This particular variety of greens cooks fast, and can turn to mush, so I watched carefully.

The well-cooked leaves were like cooked lettuce, the ribs juicy. Some less-cooked leaves still had a mustardy bite, but the rest was sweet, and so good in this recipe!

The bacon, apple cider vinegar and honey just made the cooked greens irresistible!  This will be my go-to recipe now for eating my garden-fresh greens.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is only a spring dish. You need tender baby lettuces from the garden. And we always make ours with early wild onions or ramps if we can get them. Green onions will do if you can't get either. It needs to be mild. And we don't use honey. Sorghum or just a tad of sugar.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Not just a southern dish. My Nana, then my mother made this all the time as I was growing up in the midwest. I've been making this all my adult life too. Never, never with iceberg lettuce or old lettuce. Always made with fresh greens of all kinds, most often with the first harvest from our gardens but also using spinach, or beet, dandelion, collard or whatever greens were fresh and available. Never old greens, never iceberg.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Wilted lettuce salad—made from the garden. All ingredients matter. Leaf lettuce. Bacon fat.  Vinegar. A pinch of sugar. Green onions. A chopped hard boiled egg Best with crumbled bacon. Hearty. Country fare. 
Variation is soaked salad. Italian origin I believe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: In Florida we use this recipe on garden grown lettuce like black seeded simpson.  It’s good even in summer when the lettuce starts turning a bit bitter.  My grandmother dissolved a heaping spoon of sugar in the vinegar though, not honey.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My grandmother from Missouri used to make wilted lettuce salad using romaine, the dressing bacon, vinegar, brown sugar, basically the same thing in today's recipe.  My dad gorged on it, he loved it, I loved it too. The cooking of the bacon came first, then the vinegar and brown sugar and then the romaine was tossed in until it wilted - yummy.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Pretty darn good.  Used maple bacon :)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe is so similar to my Pennsylvania Dutch nana’s Dutch Mess. Whenever we had leftover mashed potatoes, she’d fry up some bacon and add apple cider  vinegar, scallions, and iceberg lettuce chopped. Then she turn it in to Pattie’s and then fry in bacon grease . We called them potato pancakes Nana Style! Delicious!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I grew up eating this and loving it but my mom called it "Wilted Lettuce Salad." :>
I had forgotten about it until I saw this and am ON it ...

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
My Dad used to pick leaf lettuce from the garden, fry some bacon and make his version of this salad.
Years ago I did a little research and found that this salad originated in France. Made sense as my Dad's maternal grandfather was born in France and emigrated to America when he was young.
Just for fun, I tried making his version from my memory. Wasn't a hit. Maybe that's why my Mom never made it for the whole family and he always made just enough for himself.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I use fresh lettuce from the garden, green onions and fresh radishes. A splash of Dijon mustard in the dressing adds flavor.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I checked out this recipe, wondering if it was like my Southern mother's wilted lettuce recipe (made with fresh lettuce) and realized it was exactly the same thing. "Kilt" refers to the hot bacon drippings being tossed with it, which wilts the fresh lettuce somewhat (but would be somewhat gross with already wilted, old lettuce).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Oh!  My Mom made "wilted lettuce salad" all of the time in the 60s and 70s.  We loved it, and now my husband does too.  I think that my grandmother learned about this in the early 20th C. when she emigrated to Texas (because I'm pretty sure that they knew nothing about lettuce or fried green tomatoes in Moravia, where she grew up.)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written (shocking!) - used up some old lettuce from the back of the fridge.  It was delicious and satisfying. Next time I may follow the suggestion to throw in a sliced hard boiled egg.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: In Northern Ohio we used endive.  Delish.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: followed the recipe to a T and it was so gross lol

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I use endives instead of iceberg lettuce, and sugar mixed with white vinegar and warm water instead of apple cider vinegar/honey.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This recipe seems more like the wilted lettuce side dish I grew up with than the more recognizable wilted lettuce salad, where warm bacon vinaigrette is poured over fresh greens (leaf lettuce or spinach), then served immediately. We ate wilted lettuce as a cooked side dish (like fried cabbage or stewed tomatoes). The chopped lettuce is cooked (wilted) for 4-5 minutes in the pan with hot bacon grease/vinegar, so you need a lettuce with some backbone, like iceberg, or you'll just get a mushy mess.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I live in Southern California, so I substituted habaneros for the scotch bonnets and cilantro for the culantro. It is absolutely delicious. As with most hot sauces, wear gloves while handling cut peppers or you will be sorry! The Times should add a note about gloves to the recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Scotch bonnets have the best flavor, but that being said, this recipe does not flatter them.  The recipe as is, with 'seeds intact' is unbearably hot without offering the nice flavor of the chilis.  I tried a second batch, removing the seeds, and the sauce was better.  The best way to use scotch bonnet in a soup is not with this sauce but rather to put 1-3 whole chilis into the pot, without breaking them up.  Remove before eating.  Scotch bonnets can be frozen whole and last for a year or more.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Cleaning up the garden and using what I have. I used 15 red jalapenos (which were quite spicy for jalapenos) and 3 poblanos. Very tasty! It's not just hot; it has a great flavor profile. I will make this again...

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can’t wait to make this with my Armageddon peppers!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I can easily find culantro at Asian markets (sometimes labeled "recao"). But I never see scotch bonnets in NJ and we generally have access to harder-to-find ingredients . Even my local Caribbean market doesn't sell them fresh. I've always substituted habaneros out of necessity. Besides growing them, where have my fellow heat junkies found fresh scotch bonnets? Hesitant to order produce online, I've been burned with rotten deliveries at not so hot prices (puns intended).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Bree LaFin I get scotch bonnets at the farmer’s market in NYC’s Union Square. I don’t know where in NJ you are, but I’ve also gotten them at the Sunday market in Red Bank, and I suspect you might be able to find them if you have a decent-sized farmer’s market near you.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Very tasty! Ended up deseeding my scotch bonnets, 
and used only around 6 or 7 with this recipe, but otherwise sticker with the recipe. Extremely pleased with the results.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Love it! I grew my own scotch bonnets and scorpions this year, and this was the perfect use for 18+ of them. Yes, wear gloves! It is hot...but that's the point.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
mmm yummy yummy
big delicious 

i eat
i get happy

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Roll that dough into a roughly rectangular shape & cut the cakes in squares. No messing w/ scraps of dough or overworking the dough. Tastes just as good as a square.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I’m 75 and remember my great grandmother’s tea cakes*** at her home in Mississippi.  I am naturally hazy on the precise ingredients, though I watched them being made many times; the big surprise to me in the NYT recipe is the sour cream.
It is no exaggeration to say that these tea cakes, warm and slightly soft from the oven, were my first “favorite food.”
  *** Note: the tea cakes were made by my great grandmother’s cook, “Willie B,” and it is quite likely that the recipe used came from her.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The story made me want to bake these. The weirdnesses in the recipe (including the seemingly random prep/bake times, surprisingly hot oven, and discrepancy between baking powder in the description and baking soda in the ingredients) make me think I should find someone else's recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Now that I’ve made them, I highly recommend. I swapped in Greek yogurt for SC added more fresh nutmeg to my taste. To get the domed cookies I dropped by 1T scoop and slightly flattened them before baking ( way easier than rolling out) and baked at 350 F for about 18 mins. Terrific!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: It’s gotta be 350 F. My search of other tea cake recipes show baking soda with acid like sour cream or buttermilk so I think that is the ticket here. If you are swapping ingredients consider this chemistry.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: 450 is too high. It got burnt in the 450 F temperature  but it tasted  okay if not great.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: cookies burned at 450 and didnt rise as high as in  photo

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The article notes that Jocelyn Delk Adams of Grandbaby Cakes adds powdered freeze-dried raspberries, which color the dough pink.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written and used a 2” round cookie cutter.  I must have rolled them thinner than called for because I ended up with 60 gorgeous little cakes.  They baked in only 8 minutes.  I completed the cakes in 90 minutes; some of that time was spent getting used to rolling dough and cutting the cakes (lots of dusting flour is the key).  Delicious with coffee and I imagine tea, but a bit bland otherwise.  I’ll make these again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Should I use baking powder or baking soda?  And what is the correct bake time?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I imagine that with the sour cream, baking soda is the correct riser.  That combo, baking soda + something acidic, makes any type of biscuit, cake or pancake very tender and light.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I just made these. After reading the other comments, I baked them at 375º for 12 minutes, and they were perfectly done. However, they were pretty bland. I think this may be one of those recipes that speak to memory as much as taste. I made a chocolate glaze and dipped the tops in, because white cake with chocolate frosting was a childhood favorite, and I liked them much better.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I believe it would be more like 5 minutes to gather and measure all the ingredients prior to mixing, beating and rolling! 
BTW, I really wish most of the recipes out there were more realistic regarding prep time. Not specifically faulting this paper as I find most of published recipes seem to be exceedingly short on prep time. Best to read the through the directions a couple of times prior to starting.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Should the temperature be 350 degrees? Which most cookies are baked at.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Please reduce oven temperature or many will have burnt cookies as other stated.

author: Korsha Wilson
