commentBody: I have no idea how this recipe makes 18 cakes - I got 3 dozen and, no, I was not rolling the dough to 1/3 the listed 1/2" thickness (I used a ruler).    Otherwise, they were quick to pull together with ingredients on hand.  If I make again I think I'll add lemon zest.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Jeez, factor of 2, not 3...    I rolled to 1/2", not 1/4".

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: 450 degrees was much too hot.  My first batch burned.  I cooked the following batches for eight minutes.  Unfortunately, I found the tea cakes to be dry and bland.  I would not recommend making them.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My great auntie made these for us in little molded muffin tins.  In our favorite version she added cocoa to the batter for chocolate tea cakes.  If there is any food that fills me with nostalgia, it is tea cakes.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
450 F seems unlikely
change to "open mouth of a 4-ounce mason/canning jar'?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Followed the recipe since I’m not an expert baker .
Big disappointment ! Unpalatable 
First batch cooked had a very dark brownish color underneath , so I lowered the temperature for the second batch.The cookie did not look, height wise , as in the picture .Those « cookies » are tasteless. The cookies taste NOTHING!unlike in the description , this doesn’t even remotely come close to scones.
I will try to salvage them with some icing/glaze.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Instead of rolling and cutting them out (something I avoid whenever possible), I used a one-ounce disher, resulting in 33 cookies. Based on all the notes below I baked them at 375 for 11 minutes, and they came out well. They have a mild but lovely flavor from the vanilla and nutmeg, and I bet they will be fab with a mug of cocoa.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Threw them out. Burnt on bottom and tasted bland. Should have read the comments before trying instead of relying on the recipe creator.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
As another reader pointed out, something's off with this recipe: Prep time given as 5 minutes when beating the butter and sugar alone calls for 5 minutes?
Cook time given as 55 mins when the instructions say to bake for 12 mins? 
A 450 degree oven? 
On a different note, I just started reading Nora Neale Hurston's book, Their Eye's Were Watching God, a classic of the Harlem Renaissance. Wondered about the name "Tea Cake" for one of the male characters. Thanks for the historical background!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: As a kid, my grandmother would always have tea cakes on the table when we visited (North Florida).  It was a treat! I do not know all of her ingredients and I am sure there are multiple versions. There were two different ingredients for her tea cakes. She did not use sour cream, rather she used sour milk (after milking the cow) and cane syrup for sweetener. As you might imagine, the syrup yielded a rather robust flavor.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Lower temperature to 400º or 350º?  Perhaps use a slightly larger cutter.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Okay. Not worth the effort.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My daughter wanted to make these, and they were better than I expected. Didn’t look like cakes — looked like biscuits. The nutmeg gave it an interesting flavor, and we used Siggis vanilla yogurt instead of sour cream, which I assume sweetened it up. Would make an interesting base for a strawberry shortcake situation. Also did 350 degree oven based on comments. My 5 year old enjoyed!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I would bet that like any jam, you could microwave the tamarind paste for thirty seconds or so and it would be perfectly spreadable.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: There's Tamarind Pulp (sold in hard blocks, found in most South Asian, Southeast Asian, and some Latine grocers), Tamarind Concentrate (watery like juice), and the Tamarind Paste called for here, which has a jam-like consistency. You can easily make the paste from the Tamarind Pulp (which might be easier to find) by soaking a chunk of the pulp in hot water, then straining. Hot Thai Kitchen has a great overview of all this: https://hot-thai-kitchen.com/making-tamarind-paste/

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: For those dealing with gluten issues -  I use Bob's Redhill, King Arthur, Cup for Cup, or Better Batter gluten free flours. Use a measure of 130 grams for a cup.  The real trick is allowing the batter to rest fir 30 minutes before baking. No one can guess my baked goods are gluten free. I learned these tips from Chef Alina on FB.  Good luck.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: You want the milk to curdle. It is a vegan substitute for buttermilk.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I made this for my husband’s birthday and it was a big hit. I made as written except I didn’t have the whole spices so I used the ground spices I had on hand in same amounts. I was also worried about using 2tablespoons of baking powder I thought it might be an error so I used 1 tablespoon. The cake rose fine, was moist and delicious. The tamarind’s tartness played beautifully with the sweet frosting. I also added a squeeze of lemon juice to the frosting. This recipe is a keeper!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I made this cake with dairy and it was fantastic.
I used buttermilk and butter
The tamarind paste I found at my local store was very thin and liquidy. I was concerned it wouldn’t make a good filling but I spread it on my cake layers and it combined with the cake to make a delicious middle layer.
I also used a bit of the tamarind paste in the icing. The icing is quite sweet and the tart tamarind helped. 
My family all suggested a cream cheese frosting instead. 
I’m making this again !!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This cake sounds delicious and I would love to make it but I am wondering the 1/2 cup of tamarind paste gets added to the batter-- at the same time as the oil, applesauce and soy milk mixture? Also, if you don't need to make the cake vegan, can you substitute buttermilk for the soy milk and vinegar mixture?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: See Step 7.  However, I have never found a tamarind paste that is spreadable on its own.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The tamarind paste is spread between the layers right before frosting (see step 7). I missed it the first time reading through the recipe, too. And I would imagine buttermilk would work as a substitute for the vinegar & soy milk.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Would oat milk work as well as soy milk?

Thank you for posting a cake recipe that does not use eggs -- much appreciated!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Kalustyan's sell Calabash Nutmeg. shop local!( if you are in NYC-or online at: foodsofnations.com)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Just made this for Thanksgiving and it was excellent!  I used 1 1/2 teaspoons regular nutmeg in place of the calabash nutmeg.  I will definitely be making this again but next time will increase the cayenne and add even more tamarind between the layers.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: How would I substitute the soy milk for buttermilk? Could I remove the soy milk and apple cider vinegar and sub 1:1 with buttermilk?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: For those of us who don't want to go the whole nine yards and grind the spices, how much regular ground nutmeg, cloves, and cardamom?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have the same question!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I subscribe to NYT Cooking to learn about re spices, ingredients, and techniques. This recipe hit all the boxes, and is one of the most unusual they print. Not being Vegan, Gluten-Free, or willing to buy new versions of things I have, I used only grated nutmeg, regular cardamom, and whole milk for the cake. Loved not needing egg for texture. It’s a huge amount of batter.
   The Vegan Frosting was worth the investment in cashew butter. It really takes some time, but a delightful result!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made, by request, for my 10 year old's birthday. I reduced the cayenne to a heaping 1/4 t, and was nervous about the 2T of baking powder, so I used about 1 1/2 T. I would use the full amount of baking powder next time. I also reduced the sugar to 200 g which was good. The batter looked very oily in the pan, but turned out great. The tamarind "puree" I found was very thin and not spreadable. We added it to the cream cheese frosting - we're not fans of buttercream - and it was delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can I replace the soy milk with whole cow’s milk, and if so, any issue with curdling with the apple cider?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These are the recipes that make me miss working with gluten, but I’m gonna try it with a gf mix because I can’t pass up tamarind in a spice cake. That’ll be my only substitute. Here goes

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Let us know how it goes! I too need to often try GF subs in baking.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: What is “vegan butter” and would real milk work in lieu of the soy?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: There are a few excellent brands of vegan butter that substitute perfectly, in my experience, for dairy butter. Miyoko's is my favorite, and Trader Joe's vegan butter is also good.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Nutritional information states a serving of this cake has 200 carbs.
Two hundred carbohydrates.

(I'll regain consciousness momentarily)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Either vinegar or lemon juice will curdle vegan milk just like cow’s milk.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: 1/2 teaspoon of cayenne sounds like alot.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
We prefer desserts that are less sweet-tasting and use less sugar. Used Ermine Frosting, which uses less sugar and has a slightly nutty taste. Paired with the tangy element of tamarind, the cake was a hit for a family birthday. 

Made it gluten-free, no issues.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: So great flavor, and I followed recipe closely, but whoa, amount of liquid and cook time for the rice way off. Did the 20 min heat + 15 off heat (undisturbed) and rice was crunchy. Another 0.5 cup water, 5 min, twice, then getting frustrated so put 1 cup water for 5 more minutes. Then ANOTHER cup and another 5 minutes and turned up the heat. Disappointing results—no fluffy, all mushy 😑.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@Ilsa K. I hate to suggest this…but is there a chance that you didn’t put a lid on the pot? A tight one, which would’ve kept all the water from boiling away?

Your saga (which is definitely disheartening!) sounds like exactly what would happen if a person was boiling rice in an uncovered pot.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: thats what I was thinking, or too high of heat. I fillowed exactly and it was the perfect amount of liquid and time.@Maria in Minneapolis

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I think recipe should specify the amount of water to add to chicken bouillon and boil, prior to step 3. Sounds good and very simple. Nice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The Tomato Mixture (which includes 2 tomatoes, a large bell pepper, and 3 chilis pureed together) added immediately after the bouillon would presumably be enough liquid to dissolve the 1 tablespoon of bouillon without additional water or a pre-boiling step - that's why bouillon "granules" are specified (not cubes).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: what amount of water does the recipe suggest? two tomatoes do not seem to be sufficient amount of liquid for 2 1/4 cups of thirsty (basmati) rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The tomato mixture added in Step 4 includes 2 C water. In Step 3 you only stir to coat the rice, then add the liquid in Step 4.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
*Use 3c. of water*
As written, there is not enough water in this recipe. I relied on my go-to rice cooking method: 1. Rinse rice until water runs clear
2. Use 1 1/3c. water for every cup of rice.  I cut the recipe by about half: 1 1/2 c. rice, so kept 2 c. water in step 1. 
3. Bring to a boil before reducing to low simmer and covering. Cooked as directed (20mins on, 15mins off). 
The dish was perfect. Will be making every week!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Never heard that West Africans use olive oil! West African cuisine typically relies more on locally available oils like palm oil, groundnut (peanut) oil, and coconut oil.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The flavor of this rice dish was excellent. Only used one Serrano chili and it was way too spicy. Recipe is correct is saying enough for a crowd. I’d make again with another adjustment to the heat.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: i read the recipe several times but could not figure out the amount of liquid -- soup, water? which is key to a good rice dish

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The tomato mixture prepped in Step 1, then added to the rice in step 4, includes 2 cups of water (in addition to pureed tomatoes etc.)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I personally use more water than this, but one option is to soak the basmati rice first (as is done in a lot of S Asian cooking), and then drain it. It will need less water.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The two cups of water should be included in the tomato mixture part of the recipe. Otherwise it's a great recipe, make sure to stir the rice instead of letting it sit, as the recipe says. Because I ended up with half crunchy half done rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have a much simpler jollof rice recipe that does not require blending the tomato mixture. Saute the onion as in step 2, then add the tomato mixture ingredients without blending first, then rice. If rice isn't completely cooked in 35 minutes (I use brown rice that takes longer), simply cover it for additional time after making sure the base mixture is boiling before adding the rice, as the recipe recommends. I leave the pot on the burner so residual heat aids the cooking.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: If you can find it at a Mediterranean market, try it with SMOKED basmati rice!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Re the (missing?)amount of water: some say it’s mentioned in either step 2 or step 4. I do not see any additional liquid mentioned anywhere. Please advise. TIA.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Yes, there's 2 c. of water in the tomato mixture, but even with the other veg, that does not seem like enough water for over 2 c. of rice. Is the chicken bouillon supposed to be reconstituted?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I found that the rice was not fully cooked and started burning.  Maybe needs more water.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
VERY flavorful. I omitted the cayenne pepper since there already are two whole serranos and one whole habanero in the recipe, and the jollof is still pleasantly hot. 
I also heeded the suggestion of a commenter and added more water than specified in the recipe, but I went too far in the other direction and ended up with very moist rice. Next time, I’ll go with the 2 cups and see where I end up. 
Finally, 20 minutes on medium-low resulted in something like a tahdig on the bottom of the dish. If you like that, no problem. If you want uniformly cooked rice, turn it down to low.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Rice started to burn… .  Seems like totally insufficient liquid.  Rescued it into another pot and added water.  Next time I will pre soak the rice or try even lower heat.  When using a cast iron pot, the temperature stays high for longer, perhaps that explains the difference?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Hans Deseure same thing happened to me. Even after switching pots the rice was still catching on the bottom of the pan

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I wouldn’t make this recipe again. The rice burnt twice as there is not enough liquid in the recipe. I am a confident cook and managed to save it but the rice did not cook uniformly through the recipe without stirring it. I halved the chilli but it was still nice and spicy. Good flavours despite having to babysit the cooking process.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Perfection! Very straightforward preparation, yielding very flavorful results. This is a recipe I will save and make many times over. I cooked this in an enameled cast iron pan, and stirred twice during cooking only because I am terrible at judging the simmering temp for rice. It still came out great. I also subbed unrefined palm oil for half of the olive oil. I highly recommend trying that, for a little smoky funk.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I would cut back the amount of oil used and also think 20 minutes wasn't enough cooking time - rice came out a bit toothy. I also subbed out Poks Jollof Rice seasoning for all the spices - delicious!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I followed the recipe amounts exactly, and used one of my wide le creuset pots with a lid and I also had crunchy rice after 35 minutes. I added more water and gave another 5 minutes twice, still crunchy. Then did more water and about 6 more minutes. It still probably could have used a little more time but we were hungry.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is.... ok. I went with a 14oz can of whole tomatoes because off-season tomatoes have no flavor, and I figured it could use the extra liquid anyway. Then I filled the can and added that amount of water to the blender. It's strange because the dish is still moist, the rice didn't burn, but it also didn't really cook? I soaked the rice for about 20 minutes but maybe I should have soaked it longer. Don't usually have this issue with basmati rice. The rice should be parboiled, honestly.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Even after reading the comments and adjusting my prep, this rice took several rounds of adding water and more cook time . The flavor was good though I seeded all my peppers to reduce the heat. I used brown rice , pre soaked it for 30 minutes and started with 1:1.75 rice to water ratio. Ultimately it needed at least 45 minutes and at least 1:2 cups of water per cup of rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was really really good on day one. On day two the addition of large shrimp made it sing (and me, too). Using 2c of water, I followed the recipe exactly with the exception of using two large heirloom tomatoes and Better Than Bouillon rather than granules. Used a large round le Cruset baking pan (which ended up completely full) and was pleasantly surprised that the rice was cooked perfectly using the specified times. I’m glad it made lots!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Just remade this and used parboiled rice instead. Came out perfectly cooked, tender without being mushy. You simply cannot use basmati for jollof rice.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: When I prepare this again I will reverse the olive oil and lime juice measurements to 1/4 cup olive oil and 1/2 cup lime juice. Olive oil at 1/2 cup thickly coats everything and pools in the bottom of the bowl and lime juice is nearly imperceptible at the original measures.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
@Bill S.

I was thinking same as I read the recipe, or maybe 1:1 oil/acid.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: You could also start with boiling dried black-eyed peas, since black-eyed peas cook really fast (no soaking required). A good way to control the amount of salt.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Given food restrictions, omitted lime juice and chiles but with a very light EVOO this was great with the other ingredients, a bit of juice from a few tomatoes and diced raw zucchini instead of red peppers (also restricted). For us, no need for s/p. Next time might throw in a second herb and red onion and would then let it sit longer. A great use of b.e.p., home cooked or canned, as a light supper with cornbread, corn or the cob or, to mix things up, grilled pita or a traditional flatbread.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: @Susan C. Instead of adding another herb, what about mixing in cut-up kale  or spinach or other light salad greens?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Made as written with fresh ingredients, only exception was using black eyed peas from dried. Salad was soupy, oily and bland. I removed over 1/2 cup of liquid & I should've removed more. If I make again, I will use dressing ratio like Samin Nosrat's Salad-e Shirazi (or just add black eyed peas to that recipe). People did eat it at the potluck but def not a crowd pleaser.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
well, at least that way you got lots of “test tasters” and feedback.

I am reminded of my mother, who told me not to make a dish for the first time for guests — and presumably to share at potlucks. But, as the name says, its’ a “pot-luck.”

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
@Deb V. 
How could it possibly have been soupy? Did you not drain your beans after cooking?
What liquid were you removing?
If it was bland, that suggests you undersalted your beans when you cooked them.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except for starting with dried black eyed peas and cooking them in an Instant Pot (if anyone needs it, stir one cup black eyed peas with three cups water or broth in the insert, then cook on high pressure for nine minutes, then natural release for fifteen minutes). Very bright flavor and great as a side with grilled tofu.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Thanks so much for the recipe!  I ate this salad often in Brazil, could never get it to taste quite right once back in Canada. Hadn’t realized it was originally a West African dish, but that totally makes sense, so much Brazilian cooking is based on or adapted from what enslaved people there made.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Le the peas sit in the marinade all night, and finish the salad the next day. Be sure to add garlic to the marinade.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I make this with dried black eyed peas and love it so much. Sometimes I add in extra herbs if I have them in the kitchen and I blitz them into the salad dressing with an immersion blender.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: To all those complaining of blandness, as with all cooking, you can adjust the seasoning with salt, lime juice, herbs, and chile to to taste. If you find this dish bland, you are under seasoning it, period.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Swapped the amount of oil and lime juice (1/2 cup lime juice and 1/4 cup olive oil). I seasoned the vinaigrette before adding any of the ingredients with 2 cloves of minced garlic, 1/4 tsp of grated fresh ginger, salt and black pepper. Make sure you like the flavor of the vinaigrette before adding the rest of the ingredients. Also important to emulsify the vinaigrette with a good whisking so it won’t separate and pool oil at the bottom of the bowl. I de-seeded the cucumbers hoping to introduce less water and left the salad to marinate for a few hours in the fridge, mixing it every now and then. Used flat leaf parsley, 2 jalapeños, canned black eyed peas (which significantly decreases the labor involved and ensures the beans are firm). Lots of prep time but it’s delicious and nutritious. Make it your own!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I found this a bit bland.  Might need to add some additional condiments to up the flavor.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
@Megan
Try adding more seasoning

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Use less oil and more lime

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: This was a disappointing recipe for me. I made it as directed using fresh tomatoes and peppers from our garden and it turned out bland. I feel like it’s missing some key ingredient/spices.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I used fresh black eyed peas, cut the olive oil in half, and substituted jalapeños since I didn't have serranos... soooo good!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: This is really good! My husband and I loved it, kids thought it was fine, but it’s a hit at potluck dinners. It reminds me of tabouli!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: If you google the recipe the ingredients are almost always the same.  I would say it is a healthy salad that makes you feel well after eating it, but simple in flavor.  I added garlic, cumin, smoked paprika, cilantro, red onions and red wine vinegar, and omitted the tomatoes and cukes, but the flavors remained both complex.  I added salt and pepper and oil and lime and vinegar at the plate to keep the salad fresh for leftovers.  Would make again because it really does feel healthy, not everything we eat makes you feel that way 😉

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: So you didn't really make this recipe with all the substitutions.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Drain both peas and veg well. I salted the chopped veg separately in a strainer and got a half cup liquid out. Don’t like soupy salad.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Just made it as written. Very good. Next time I will cook the dried BEPs instead of using canned. My canned ones were a little too firm for my liking. I felt they were slightly undercooked. I didn’t let the salad sit before trying because I was too hungry. I heated up some leftover white rice and put a couple of spoonfuls of the salad on top. A fabulous and filling lunch,

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Holy smokes!   So much more than the sum of its parts.  I served it to rave reviews and it held for a couple days worth of Can’t-Wait-For-It lunches.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I used Pinto and Turtle Beans.  Like some of the other commentors I felt the dish as very bland.  There was really no seasoning taste at all.  Ive been experimenting with ingredients to add flavour.  I addded 1/2 teaspoon each of cumin and chile powder.  That was not enough, so I added fresh mint and basil.  That seemed to do the trick - I don't have any but I think fresh cilantro would be a good addition.  I would reconsider the curly parsley

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Postive--good mix of vegetables, very healthy, great pick for farm season in NJ.  Negative--bland!, and a little watery.  (I seeded the cucumber, but not the tomatoes.) When making the dressing, definitely add your favorite spices for pizzazz.  (For me, cumin and a pinch of cayenne.)  And it seems a little fussy to have scallions AND shallots, and neither added enough flavor.  I will use red onions next time.  Also, I think I will sub cilantro for the parsley to see if that helps.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I used Glory canned black eyed peas, which are seasoned and worked well. Since they’re salted, no further salt was needed, as well.  But for my tastes, the amounts of scallions and the shallot were excessive.  I’d reduce the scallions in particular by half, or more. It also might help to de water the cucumber, but of course that would increase the saltiness. On the whole, a very nice summer salad that works well with sliced ham.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: This salad is excellent without the oil.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Thank you, thank you, thank you!!! We try to avoid added oil, so I am thrilled to see your comment. Did you make any other changes, beyond leaving out the oil?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
@RenoRose
Try lengthening time spent emulsifying olive oil, lime juice and parsley (first step). I’ve made this recipe twice and ended up with sore arm after step one but no olive oil collected at the bottom of the bowl.  I can’t imagine leaving out the oil!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I made as directed but used Italian flat leaf parsley because I prefer it.  I liked it, but agree it needs a bit more of a punch…I don’t think the lime juice quite carries the acidity. I added a few splashes of red wine vinegar. Easy and so nutritious. Parsley is a superfood, so was happy to find this recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: My mother made all kinds of bean salads during the summer. For her dressing it was always 3 things : 1 part oil (EVO usually), more than 1 part vinegar, less than 1 part sugar or honey, clove or two crushed garlic, and s/p...then she'd put that in a small sauce pan and bring to a simmer to dissolve the sugar, she'd let it cool for a few min, then adjust s/p or sweetness. Boiling helps marry all the ingredients, you never have a salad that seems oily or acidic. Really marinates the beans.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Sounds good but I much prefer the Gullah Geeche version from SoCarolina! Pretty much the same except no Serranos -- red pepper instead and has a light amount of cooked rice! Yum ...

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
“Sounds good but I much prefer…”

Did you try this one?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I I have been making a version of this for years. Except I use fresh salsa from the dairy case at the store as well as lots of lime juice and no oil at all.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Used cannellini beans and Megan’s Aleppo seasoning instead of jalapeño pepper

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I did as one commenter suggested and swapped the lime juice/olive oil amounts. Still, my husband and I found this dish to be totally 'meh.' No flavor whatsoever. I added crumbled feta in an attempt to give it any kind of flavor at all, but ultimately the entire salad wound up in the trash. It's especially disappointing given the amount of time required slicing and dicing.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Made for a potluck picnic. I used cilantro because it was in my CSA share and I needed to use it up,  macho chiles and lemon (instead of Serranos and lime because that's what I had). I also added a couple of dashes of Aleppo pepper. Next time I would add the cucumbers and tomatoes just before serving, or drain them for a while before adding, and maybe some cumin. But I'll make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody:
It seems that salads are very forgiving!

Thanks for these suggestions.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Made as written and it turned well, but next time I'll use a lot less (or no) oil and add some other herbs, maybe basil, and garlic or cumin.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I added extra black pepper, minced garlic and cumin. I also tossed 3-4 times over 2 hours,

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Easy, excellent, and holds up well in the fridge! The lime adds a nice bright flavour. I halved the olive oil and didn’t miss it.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering if lime juice is too mild for a vegetable salad. It works so well in fruit salad but I had to add red wine vinegar and lemon juice and lemon zest to pep it up.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: The beauty of this recipe is the simplicity of the ingredients. It is the perfect side for heavy flavored entrees. We had it with buttermilk marinated chicken legs that were cooked on our kamado grill with pecan wood. You have to like chopping veggies for this one.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: My sister has been bringing this salad to family events for years.  She calls it Red-neck Caviar.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Used drained black eyed peas from dried.  Used 1/3 cup olive oil.  It’s delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: Halved the recipe and cut olive oil and lime juice to 3TB each and thought it was perfect. I bought a Serrano for this but it’s lost in my fridge so used a few shakes of black & red pepper blend and it had a nice kick. Looking forward to remaking, with Serrano, to get that herbaceous vibe. Was a great side for oven-fried catfish.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
commentBody: If you have a garden full of other types of tomatoes like I do, you can use them in this recipe.  But, you should sweat them first.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Sounds delicious and I can't wait to try it.  But I have to comment that crushing the saffron threads in a paper towel strikes me as a very bad idea.   A significant amount of the saffron powder would become caught in the textured paper towel and lost.  As one of the most expensive spices, that seems like an unnecessary waste.  I would use parchment paper.  Even a clean sheet of printer paper likely would be preferable to a paper towel.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Made this and followed the recipe as written.

The saffron sauce is delightful. Worth it to find some higher quality saffron to have that lovely flavor come through the entire dish - Penzey’s Afghanistan saffron was perfect.

For two people, the recipe makes plenty of dumplings/filling. Make sure to dice the shrimp up as fine as you can (almost to a paste), so that when it cooks and plumps a bit, it’s still easy to manipulate into the dumpling wrappers.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I've been to Lapis while visiting Washington--a great spot!  Where does one source the wrappers?  --could wonton wrappers be substituted?  or are Afghan dumpling wrappers something else again?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Use a mortar and pestle for the saffron.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was phenomenal! My only note is that my sauce did not come out the same color as the picture; mine looked more like the color of the dumpling wrappers. It was still delicious but not very beautiful. Next time, I might try to bloom the saffron first in hot water or add some turmeric to the sauce to give it more vibrancy.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: By far, the most delicious thing I have made off of New York Times cooking. My guests were incredulous on how flavorful, nuanced and decadent this dish was. Worth the effort.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I was underwhelmed with this recipe. The sauce was too heavy for the shrimp dumplings. It seemed too rich and fatty overall.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’d love to also have your recipe for the handmade dumpling wrappers! I’m looking forward to making this dumpling recipe. Thank you!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I order this dish whenever I have lunch at Lapis.  I'm anxious to try making them myself.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Very yummy and were a hit. I made half with shrimp and half with pork sausage cause my wife doesn't eat shrimp. Still worked well in my opinion. If your family isn't big on sauces consider halving the sauce quantity. I have sooooo much leftover.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: can this be frozen?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: A coffee grinder works well for crushing saffron, as does a mortar and pestle.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I don’t see why you couldn’t dissolve the saffron in the wine, instead of crushing it.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’ve frozen the dumplings. Can I freeze the sauce?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made the dumplings and froze them. Can I freeze the sauce?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: are these Shanghai or Hong Kong wrappers?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe! I put them in a steamer basket rather than boil them to preserve the flavor. I'd also suggest to not overcook the shrimp (maybe two minutes). They are going to finish cooking and meld with the other ingredients while steaming.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have been crabbing and eating crabs in South Carolina for 75 years. The Old Bay seasoning in this recipe will overwhelm the sweet, delicate flavor of the crab and you might as well be eating cod. Our quick method to prepare crab was to soak white bread with a little milk, butter, eggs, and mayonnaise, add seasoning to taste and stir in the crab.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: There's only 1 t. of Old Bay mixed with a pound of crabmeat, not excessive but you could always start with a smaller amount and add more if desired.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: On the west coast,  this can be made with rock crabs. Several species abound and one can catch them off a pier or jetty w/out a license.  All you need is a crab net, some bait, and a free afternoon.  They're larger than blues,  way smaller than Dungeness, and delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My grandmother would put breadcrumbs and melted butter on top then put in the over and broiler.  Have to use real crab backs though,  so much better !

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Tampa  has it's own deviled crab  specialty.  The ingredients look basically the same but in Tampa they are made into a croquette and deep fried.  It is not something you make at home but Cuban restaurants serve them and it is very tempting to add one to your order, splash it with hot sauce and eat it while your meal is being prepared.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: They’re so much better when you catch them yourself!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Where's the coconut sour cream recipe to accompany?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Is there any reason this wouldn't work (in favor of something quicker) to chop up the cauliflower, then roast it with the sauce?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Outstanding. I quartered it to serve like a lettuce wedge and drizzled pan drippings over it. Also, I had a bit of glaze leftover and brought it to the table. The whole shebang disappeared. It's definitely a party piece that guests will rave about.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Made with jalepeno since that’s what I had. Used the whole habanero, seeded. Pretty good spice level. Starts as very mild, seems too little. By the time I finished a slice, it all came together perfectly. Will make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: You can simplify and expedite this recipe by using the excellent Walkerswood jerk paste, available in mild and spicy. I used to make my own, but this is as good or better, and is now my go-to. https://walkerswood.com/

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Jah Neekay do you add anything to it to make it “glaze-y” or just brush on the paste and call it a day?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Jess Add some sugar or maple syrup.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I cook the whole cauliflower head in our Big Green Egg smoker and finish it off under the broiler in the kitchen.  The glaze, enhanced by the smokiness from the grill, is absolutely delicious.  It's an easy summer grill recipe for us.  If there are leftovers, I add some chicken stock and turn it into cauliflower soup for the next day.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Two suggestions. First, just buy Walkerswood jerk seasoning. It’s as good as anything I’ve had in Jamaica or made myself. You can add sugar to it and thin it out with water to make it a glaze. Second, if you put this under the broiler, only the very top of the cauliflower will get blackened. For the second step, I would just raise the oven temperature to 425-450 and roast it until it’s browned.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Would the recipe leave extra glaze either unused (or in the skillet after basting) that could be drizzled on at the table?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was the first NYT recipe that simply did not work well for me. A total flop. Most importantly, though...one tablespoon of salt has to be a mistake. I started adding a tablespoon and then stopped halfway. It was ridiculously salty...and I'm a salt lover.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This was DELICIOUS. My family doesn’t like spicy foods, so used plain sunflower oil instead of Chile oil, added a couple teaspoons of gochugaru instead of fresh spicy pepper, but otherwise added all the ingredients. Served with coconut rice, and the residual sauce was delicious on that as well! Will definitely make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can anyone provide a recipe for the coconut sour cream that's recommended as an accompaniment please?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I’ve seen it in stores. Could probably also use coconut yogurt

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My broiler is under the oven and isn't nearly high enough to stand up a head of cauliflower.  Suggestions?  I could just put it back in the oven with the glaze.  Or maybe cut it in half top to bottom and lay each half down which I think would fit in the broiler space.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Halfing it would work. The recipe calls for it whole as it's quite a dramatic presentation however, the flavor won't change with halfing it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Ick! I love Gregory Gourdet, and ET is my fav cookbook of all time--it's refreshingly modern and comforting. This is the ONLY recipe that was a complete failure in the whole book. Burned, unevenly seasoned, and somehow still waterlogged after ALL the time and broiling.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Where’s the coconut sour cream?  I made it and now can’t find it. This is an amazing dish

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Definitely the best whole head roasting process I've tried and will be replicating with other seasonings; sauce was very flavorful and plenty spicy without a Scotch bonnet or habanero but it makes so much in volume I have to think of other ways to use it up

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Delicious, love the spice.  After it was cooked, so easy to tear apart and serve as a side.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Trader Joe’s has coconut amino.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I didn't want to heat up the whole house, so I cooked the head of cauliflower in the slow cooker, then broiled it with the sauce in the toaster oven.  Still delicious.  I think an air fryer would also work well with the florets broken up.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: If you get shrimps with their heads and shells on, and don’t want the heads on the shrimp when serving, you can quickly simmer up a shrimp stock for the liquid in this. Simply bring a pot of water to boil, adding a sliced onion and the tops and bottoms of a few stalks celery.   Also in the water a teaspoon peppercorns and a few bay leaves.  While this is at a good simmer peel your shrimp, and place the heads and shells in the simmering liquid and let it go another 15-20 minutes before straining.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody:
I don’t under stand why this is so good. It is perfect for halibut.  Makes a lot, 1.5 gallons or so.  May not keep long.  Using frozen vegetables it is a pantry dish.

Jalapeños = 1 4 oz can green Chile+ some leftover Thai red chili in for 5 min
Shrimp= 1pound frozen halibut + 1/2 pound frozen red salmon in 2 inch chunks
Greens= 10 oz frozen spinach+10 oz frozen kale
Okra= 4 oz frozen chopped green beans
Stock = 2 Tbsp instant veggie boullion

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: I have to add it's nice to get some homemade file' online made in Louisiana. The best is traditionally made by the Houma Indian Nation. The smell of the homemade foraged wild and prepared in the wooden mortar is unbelievable. I often find some on etsy of ebay or I buy it when I see it at meat markets in Louisiana when I'm back there. It's so much more flavorful than the dried out massed produced stuff.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody:
Taste was great, but...
Please estimate a weight for "bunch of curly kale."  I probably used too much.
I pureed and had green paste--recommend immersion blender and chop.
Used a pound of crab and a pound of shrimp.  Could have used more.
Some people wanted it hotter--maybe 2 jalapeños or pass the hot sauce.
Some suggested adding Louisiana hot sausage.  Will try again 'cuz it tasted good but looked horrible.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious. From growing up in Louisiana there are roux-less gumbos that aren't as famous as Chicken and Andouille with a dark roux. You see them in Creole communities and in among Bayou Cajuns who fish and shrimp for a living. Instead of a flour roux okra and file' thicken the gumbo. This seems in that category. I added black pepper, red pepper, and white pepper to season everything. That's sort of the other trinity in Louisiana cooking. So good!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Not sure I would call this a Gumbo...so I added gumbo seasoning: Bay leaf, paprika, dry mustard, cumin, cayenne pepper. This gave the dish a deeper flavor profile. Loved a dish packed with super foods!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! This is an old New Orleans Creole recipe. The addition of coconut milk gives it a modern touch. I added 1/4 cup Pernod and it was wonderful! The anise flavor complements the greens and seafood perfectly. Next time I will add oysters!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: This was the first gumbo I ever had or made, so I had no reference point, but it was enjoyable. To save time, I pureed the raw greens (spinach, and Swiss chard instead of kale) in a food processor with the (light) coconut milk before adding them to the pot and simmering as per Step 1. I used 100% seafood stock and no water. I did not seed the jalapeno, and still it lacked heat, so I served with Tabasco sauce. Some acid is essential, so do serve with lemon wedges.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: This seemed pricey when I started cooking, but I made this tonight with shrimp and crab from Costco. The vegetables came from an organic farm where I belong. I added up the cost and decided that this recipe made enough for three meals for my husband and me – $5 per meal each, maybe less. My husband said it was a big winner. He is looking forward to the leftovers, which I plan to freeze. We added a lot of basil from our garden and some Louisiana hot sauce.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written but was an expensive endeavor for such a completely flavorless return. The texture was perfect though. Creamy and hearty. But jeez just a bland situation. I’ll need to rework adding seasonings today to figure out how I can salvage this. Not worth the effort.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions for okra substitute?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Looks like a chicken soup I made when my boys were very young. I threw leftover spinach in it and it turned the broth green. I told them is was Swamp Stew and they loved it. LOLOL I must try this, but not crazy about Okra.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Needs some alterations.  First, if using fresh okra, plan for additional 20 minutes of simmering prior to putting in the potatoes to let the okra break down and get soft.  Cut the spinach and kale in half.  The color of green was beautiful but too thick. Overall, seasoning is lacking.  I added 1.5 TBL of Creole seasoning which helped up the sodium and  overall spice.  After that, I actually enjoyed the dish.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody: Not too flavorful, used chicken andouille but we should have browned it, needed another layer of flavor

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
commentBody:
This is just a wonderful recipe! The roux takes a while, but the flavors are great and the result is terrific. A great way to do scallops. Think you could use both large sea scallops (which I used) or small bay scallops, but, if ymou used the latter, you'd need to cook them for a shorter time.
13 - Reply Share
Great, easy, and very flexible 2 years ago This recipe is a keeper. I've made it several times and it gets raves every time. It's also quite adaptable- tonight's edition featured leftover, shredded duck, duck broth and chicken andouille sausage instead of chicken broth and scallops. I do add 1 1/2 cups of frozen okra when the tomatoes go in - I just feel that gumbo is the reason okra exists so

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Please please please be consistent with measurements - this recipe has grams, cups, teaspoons , ounces and then asks to measure the rolls in 65 gram lots, it would be so helpful to show all 3 measurements or just be consistent with one type - thank you - I really enjoy so many of the recipes you but consistent measurements would be great.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I agree - consistent measurements are a must in bread making. I make sourdough and learned the hard way that you really must weigh all ingredients for sucessful results.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I made these tonight and they are amazing!  The roll is tender but has structure from the sweet potato. I followed the recipe.  It is very dry right now so I added about a tablespoon extra of buttermilk.  I did use my stand mixer for 8 minutes and needed to put the dough in a very warm place to help them rise.  These are excellent with some butter and a little honey, or just by themselves.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Could this be made with Gluten free flour?  Looks wonderful!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: My guess is no. Baking bread with gluten free flour is fraught with trial and error, and many times, disappointing in the extreme. Ask me how I know. However you may have some luck converting this recipe by reading (and strictly following) the guidelines given by the blogger Gluten-Free on a Shoestring. Of all the bloggers I've tried recipes from, hers are the most thoroughly and scientifically researched. She's put a huge amount of energy and time (not to mention money) formulating her recipes.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: At what stage can one stop and "hold" for a day ahead of time baking?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: After you shape the rolls and place them in the pan, cover and park in the refrigerator until you're ready to bake (1-3 days even!).  Take them out an hour or two before you're ready to bake to come to room temp and finish the rise

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Could you please give a meaurement (in cups) for the sweet potato?  My definition of "small" could vary greatly from others.  Thank you

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: The weight is provided, and is a more accurate measure.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Made these today and loved them. Followed the recipe exactly EXCEPT that I also added a sprinkling of Maldon salt along with the final brushing of butter. Highly recommend. All I could think of while eating them is how perfect they would be with a hearty bowl of groundnut stew. Thank you for this wonderful recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Do tell, what is "groundnut stew"? Surely it can't be what squirrels eat...

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: @Lolo it's an African stew made with peanut butter

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Very easy to make, these buns are visually appealing and have a perfect texture.  I think they are much better when fully cooled.  They were an excellent accompaniment to Melissa Clark's Golden Leek and Potato Soup.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: These have become a regular at our house.  I beat the sweet potato and butter together in a stand mixer, add the buttermilk and eggs and mix until combined, then switch to a dough hook, add the dry ingredients and knead for 6 minutes.  I've also taken to doing an overnight proof in the refrigerator for the second rise.  The results are consistently light, moist, and flavorful, and the color is terrific.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I am notorious for not being able to make any type of leavened bread (despite being a decent cook) and these looked so good I thought I'd give them a try! They came out perfectly! Probably didn't mix properly in the stand mixer, maybe the dough was too wet, didn't measure the balls by weight.. and still made the softest, perfect, pull apart buns. They didn't even last a day! Keeper recipe!!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe using a stand mixer and the dough hook for the 8 minute knead. It is a dry dough and rose nicely. Take the time to shape the balls and they are picture perfect. Tender and tasty

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe mostly exactly. I had 5.6 oz of leftover roasted sweet potatoes and used those instead of boiling a new batch. I made the rolls sandwich roll size instead of little dinner roll size. Sooooo goood! I can even taste a hint of sweet potato in the rolls.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have buttermilk, so I subbed out from plain yogurt, and these rolls were delicious! Fluffy and such a gorgeous color. I halved the recipe and made 9 slightly smaller rolls in a 8x8 pan (about 58 grams each roll). They got a second life a couple days later made into toasted ham and cheese sliders.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody:
THese turned out amazing! DId not get a lot of sweet potato notes in the final product but rather they were the platonic ideal of a dinner roll. Fluffy and delicious.

I didn't have any buttermilk so I used the old "whole milk + vinegar" trick and it worked fine.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I microwaved the sweet potato. My dough was dry, definitely not sticky. I added some buttermilk as suggested by another commenter. So I think boiling the sweet potato would add more moisture to make a stickier dough. We’ll see how the texture is and how they taste but they look perfect.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody:
Is the warmth from the sweet potato mixture enough to activate the yeast? In which case, you need to add the yeast while that warmth is there, since there’s no warm water to activate?
Just asking, because I struggle with yeast which seems terribly finicky to me, but I love yeast breads.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Instant yeast will be activated anyway - it will just take longer in a cool dough.  Good luck!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with sourdough starter instead of yeast? Would it work?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I've made it with kefir and it works great!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: After shaping each roll, I dip each in melted butter and then in a mixture of sugar and cinnamon. Before placing them in the pan. They make wonderful breakfast rolls.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe. I would definitely advise to add more salt than suggested. If you are questioning it, you can always take a pinch of the dough when you’re mixing it. I also was short on buttermilk so I substituted plain Greek yogurt partially and it was great!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I have made these many times and they are very riff-able. I have used pumpkin instead of sweet potato, added roasted garlic, and I usually cook the sweet potato in the buttermilk and butter, and I will add a sprig of thyme if I have it. The buttermilk will split but when you mix everything it doesn’t matter. I usually puree with my immersion blender before adding the flour. Also! I almost always add about 1/4-1/3c of zaatar to these. And sprinkle some sesame seeds on top. Sometimes I think they could use a bit more salt, but they always get eaten up.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Is boiling the sweet potato necessary for the additional moisture or could I use a baked sweet potato?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Can I microwave the sweet potato?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Can these rolls be made ahead? Can they be refrigerated after step 7?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Generally, you can skip the proofing in a warm spot (partway through step 7) and instead cover the formed rolls and do a slow cold ferment in the fridge overnight. I do that with various bread and rolls and bread and you actually get a more complex flavor with a cold ferment.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if this dough could be made in a bread machine?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Do any of you know if these just as good the next day, when reheated? I'd like to serve them to company, but it looks like too much for me to do on a day guests are coming. Thanks!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody:
I baked these today, for Christmas dinner. 

I weighed all the ingredients. The dough was still very wet. I had to add about half a cup more of flour to get the dough to come together. It really strained my Kitchen Aid stand mixer. 

The rolls came out light, fluffy and tasty, although the sweet potato didn't stand out, taste wise. They also didn't brown much, even after baking for an additional five minutes. 

Overall consensus: Very good, but not amazing.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: The dough was very dry and wouldn't come together until I added more buttermilk. I didn't measure the extra buttermilk, rather just kept pouring more in while it was mixing until the dough felt ready to knead.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe as directed, turned out delicious. I was impressed with how well they tasted even two days later.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I found that 65 gram portions left me with about 300 grams extra, so I had to add some back to each portion. In total it was more like 85 grams each.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I cooked 70g dough for 15 mins at 375 and they were a beautiful golden brown. Unfortunately they turned out to not be completely cooked—doughy, definitely under cooked.  Next time I’ll cook 20-25 mins. Otherwise delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: @dmf Consider testing with thermometer, 195 degrees F for enriched breads like this one.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Some people put their breads in the refrigerator overnight for a slow rise. If it's winter time, I will do a slow rise too but leave it in the garage because it's usually a similar temperature. Just cover tightly with cling wrap to keep the nasties out, and you save some space in your fridge. You could probably leave the dough on a balcony or fire escape just the same if you are a city dweller and weather won't drop below freezing at night. Small kitchen strategies!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: These weee a huge disappointment. Very under-seasoned, lacking in flavor.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Mine came out looking more like biscuits than rolls? Tasted pretty good, but looked very different than the photo…

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: I used bread flour and my dough was way too dry. Added buttermilk and filtered water till it felt right and they came out amazing. Pull apart rolls have always been my baking nemesis but I took a chance with these and haza!!!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: The buns are delicious, they rise really well. Definitely add more sweet potato if that flavor was the reason you chose this recipe, it barely tasted of sweet potato (even knowing it had some in it). Still a delicious fluffy roll though!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: These are very good. Gorgeous color and lovely texture and taste. Perfect for Thanksgiving feast.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Flawless. Used honey (1.5T) because I don’t have sugar in the house. I like the 65g size. Very  nice spongy and moist bread texture.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody:
Wow, these turned out great for Thanksgiving. 
Thanks for the recipe; it was very user-friendly and easy to make for those of us who are not novice cooks.
They were fluffy and soft, and with some butter in the middle, they were outstanding.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: The texture and flavor of these rolls were wonderful and they freeze and reheat very well. Great use for leftover baked sweet potatoes. I made the dough in the food processor so it came together in a couple of minutes.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: Made double the recipe for Xmas eve. Used a drained can of yams because it was what I had on hand. I was in a time crunch. I mostly followed the recipe. They turned out lovely. Everyone loved them.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Sweet Potato Buttermilk Pull-Apart Rolls Recipe
commentBody: What could I use to replace the eggs w/out jeopardizing flavor? Flax? Applesauce?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I have to look up my family recipe for these date cookies to see how it compares. I can tell you all that we use a date paste (found in middle eastern stores) and that the dough has a fair amount of butter and zest from a tangerine or clementine. When made well they are delicious!!  Spreading the dates can be cumbersome, but using wet fingertips helps a lot.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: delicious!  Added finely chopped walnuts to the flour mixture.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Really tasty, many compliments.  Next time will not use parchment paper as the dough is too thick to spread easily - the parchment slid all over.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Paula You can use binder clips to secure the parchment paper. A trick I learned from Martha Stewart. :)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I cut the recipe in half and added about 3/4 cup of toasted pecans/walnuts to the food processor with the dates.  I also cut the down on the sugar/honey, using a total of 1/4 cup combined (for half the recipe).  The dates made it plenty sweet - I may cut down even more next time. Delicious!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Made these with half the sugar that was suggested, added walnuts to the date mixture and orange zest to the cookie dough.
I used a 9x9 tray and baked them for 30 minutes ( though another minute or two wouldn't have hurt).
They are spectacular! Very much like a fig newton and the cookie part is not too dry.
I will let the rest sit for a couple of of days to age then wrap tightly and freeze until Christmas.
That is, if I can keep my hands off them.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: how did these fair frozen?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The amount if sugar + honey seems overkill indeed, especially given the sweetness of dates. I have not made those yet, but when I do, I will half the added sugar/ honey, msybe cut down even more, and likely add some oats

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Two days from baking, and I think they are even better. Very much like a date newton. Excellent with coffee. Will happily make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
As with others this was a royal pain. I doubt I will ever make again but if I did I’d refrigerate the dough and roll it out. No need for parchment and cooking spray—just use parchment. The dough didn’t stick. 

And I agree with everyone that they are way too salty.   Plus the recipe should note that the salt is divided. I had to throw away the first batch of dates because I used the whole 2 tsp. I thought it was too much but wasn’t paying attention. 2nd time used 1/2 tsp—still too much

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: If you butter/spray your pan FIRST, then the parchment will stick to the pan and spreading the batter will be easier. We followed the recipe as is and all the salt levels were just fine for us. The only thing I did after reading the comments was freeze the first layer a little bit before spreading the date layer. Yes, it's sticky/annoyingly difficult/time-consuming, but the results are delicious! We loved the texture and flavor; this is one of those recipes where simple ingredients sing.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
For the traditional oat date bars we remember from our childhood, here’s what I found
* Crust /topping:
*            1½ cups rolled oats
* 		1½ cups flour
* 		1 cup packed brown sugar
* 		¾ teaspoon baking soda
* 		¼ teaspoon salt
* 		¾ cup butter, softened well
* Filling:
* 		¾ pound pitted dates, diced
* 		1 cup water (maybe less?)
* 		1 teaspoon lemon juice or vanilla
Same basic process as this recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Nan Dem thank you! I made this using these ingredients, in a 9” X 7” pan, baked for 40 minutes. These are the date bars we used to make as kids, from the box. Easy, delicious and brought back great memories!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Nan Dem no eggs?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I used 1/2 buckwheat flour which was nutty and delicious. Not too salty, maybe because I used kosher salt? I think fine salt would be too much. I had to roll out the dough which worked fine. The result it almost like a date scone. I will make it again!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These came out almost perfect and are delicious. I read over all the Comments here before starting. Used a 9x9 pan and started with a 14 oz. package of date paste that I’ve been moving around in my pantry for months. Could have used another 5-10 min in the oven but hopefully I can air dry them a bit. They are impressive looking little sandwiches and I’m looking forward to sharing!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I had a similar experience as others with the sticky dough and difficulty spreading it. And there was not enough dough for a 9x13 pan. Will not make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I liked these but there are some things to watch out for: the salt—too much (i use Diamond Crystal), so cut back by at least half a teaspoon; re the top layer: make those dollops regularly-spaced and  small, so that they spread more easily, as this step is a bit tricky. Freezing the bottom layer longer might also help, though it’s okay if the coverage isn’t perfect—the batter will spread. I’d also increase the date filling by half again, when I make them again, which I will.

author: Korsha Wilson
Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My dough turned out like chocolate chip cookie dough. i put parchment on counter and spread w/offset it that way..1" uncovered border-I used less than 1/2 of batter on bottom.  Date part-I let soak in the hot water and added about 1tbls extra. Processed.  More of that mixturewould have made all TOO sweet. By mistake I did not cream butter 1st but did mix up eggs before adding.  after freezer added extra dough..a good amt...cooked 40 min.  AMAZING-but sweet- only can eat about 1" bit at a time.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Wow these are a pain.  They are in the freezer now.  Spreading out the dough was one of  the most comical baking experiences in my long life.  I gave up with the spreading bit and then sprayed a large piece of parchment folded in half, rolled the sticky dough out to size.  Then laid in pan, spread the filling.  I'm doing the same with the top half.  Wish me luck.  These better be good, I'll never make them again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
-I found it easier to spread the base layer dough with my hands. 
-less “date-y” than I’d hoped, but still a very nice recipe!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: delish but you have to juggle dough in and out of fridge to get it a bit firm to manage spreading it thin enough and an offset knife helps.  1/2 of dough did not fill full bottom of pan-does not make a difference.  nothing spreads. use full amt or nearly all of filling as otherwise bars will be a bit dry...  i also took handfuls of refrig. dough and flattened between my hands and laid that on top...be creative...they turned out great but would have been greater if I had used more filling..

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The dough is so sticky. There may be some magic I missed, but there are less stressful ways of making bars.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These are tasty but too sweet. And handling the sticky dough is annoying, to say the least.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My feeling is that the date filling was delicious, but the crust part was extremely dry.   To eat the rest of them I’ll have to whip up some whipped cream.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I must have done something wrong but can't figure out what. My batter was like peanut butter, which was hilariously impossible to spread on parchment paper and then on top of the dates. Looked like a 6-year-old had made it when it came out of the oven. Paul Hollywood would say, tastes great, but it's a bit of a mess.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Not sure what my husband did wrong. They came out very dry.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Reduced salt. Only in batter.  Even that was too much. Very difficult to spread batter layers.  End product dry and way too sweet.  I have better date bar recipes. That are also easier to make.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Had way too little batter for a 9x13-inch pan, so transferred to a 9x9 and used about 90% of the batter.  Wasn't sure how long to bake, so they came out a little dry.  Given the amount of sugar and the dates, I thought these would overly sweet, but they were a little bland.  Not overly salty as some have commented.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Seriously delicious.  My husband has celiac, so he made these using gluten-free flour.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Dough was way too stiff to spoon easily although delicious. Will add more liquid next time

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I like this recipe as I’m a savory dessert guy. Next time I make it I will increase the dough to 3 cups of flour and increase powder etc accordingly. The other suggestion someone made to roll out dough after refrigerating I will do. Not too many steps, comes together quickly.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Looking at the salt amount relative to the overall yield, I would guess that 2 tsp of Diamond Crystal kosher salt—or 1 tsp of fine sea/table/Morton kosher salt—would be an appropriate amount. I haven’t made them, so this is an abstract guess.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Per comments, I made this in a 9x9 pan, no parchment paper. Subbed in 1 cup instant steel cut oats for 1 cup flour. Really good! 9x13 pan would have made the bars more like a fig newton but I can only imagine how difficult it would be to spread the batter that thin.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I had a similarly ridiculous time trying to spread this batter on parchment paper. I oiled the paper and the spatula, then removed the parchment from the pan and asked my husband to hold down the corners of the paper...that was the only way to get a semi-even spread. The pan is in the freezer now and I am not optimistic about spreading the rest of this batter on top of the fig mixture. I won't be making this recipe again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can you make these with a hand held mixer?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Could this be made with fresh figs?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These are awesome. Cut the salt in half and mashed the dates instead of using a food processor (don’t have one). Didn’t bother to freeze the dough and they still came out awesome. These are superb warm, next time I will add oats to the dough because the flavor is vaguely reminiscent of an oatmeal raisin cookie.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: GreenPans make parchment paper obsolete, but it's certainly no easier to spread the dough out!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Any suggestions on what to substitute for honey? Would something like pomegranate syrup work?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The flavor (salty-sweet) is spot on! I had to wrestle the dough and will definitely treat it more like a pie dough next round: refrigerate and roll. (It will save much time!)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These are the best cornbread muffins I’ve ever made, & I’ve made a lot of cornbread.
