The only thing I changed was I used 1 Tbsp brown sugar & 1 Tbsp molasses instead of 3 Tbsp brown sugar because I don’t like my cornbread too sweet & molasses or sorghum syrup is in my favorite southern quickbread recipes. (To those who say southern cornbread doesn’t have sweetener traditionally, IT DOES REGIONALLY —leave those of us who prefer it that way alone, & enjoy your corn bricks in the mean time)

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is the usual modern interpretation of cornbread:  over sweetened cake. The buttermilk is traditional but true cornbread has little or no flour and sugar.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Given Chef Terry's expertise in vegan cooking, I hope he will consider offering a vegan variation of this recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I had stone ground yellow corn meal and white whole wheat flour on hand and used those, and loved the result. I'm sure grittier/heartier, but that works for me. PS, you can easily grind coarse corn meal finer in a blender or processor before measuring.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I think a version of this can be prepared more easily with a mix, one little box of Jiffy Corn Muffins (for 6 muffins or an 8x8 pan). I did so, throwing in a 4oz can of mild green chiles (drained) and then adding around 9 slivers (maybe .5 oz) of aged Gouda to the pan, placed strategically on top so each piece would have one bit of cheese. I was pleasantly surprised at the results: the added flavors worked well with the sweetened mix. It felt like it took 10 seconds to make.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@Csat
Vegetarians beware. Jiffy corn muffin mix has lard in it

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Dona Jiffy offers a vegetarian version that uses vegetable shortening instead of lard.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Judy Stark But they aren’t as tasty.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These were fabulous! After reading a few reviews, I did opt to change the sweet to 1 tbsp brown sugar and 1 tbsp maple syrup.  Delicious!  I was easily able to get 9 muffins using a standard muffin pan.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Would like to know if this could be baked in an 8" cake pan instead of 6 muffins. Only 1 cup of dry (flour + cornmeal) and 1/2 cup liquid seems like a scant amount of batter despite the 2 eggs.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @me In Texas, we would bake it in a small pre-heated cast iron skillet or oval baker.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: As soon as I saw the sugar I knew there would be the naysayers. It gets very tiresome.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: The volume is made up by the 3/4 cup corn kernels. I would definitely use muffin cups. I think it wouldn’t hold together as well in a cake pan or skillet.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Baked these after searching "buttermilk muffin" and was really pleased with how easy they were and they were great with the spicy jerk pork i made for dinner.  Warmed up great the next day  sliced in half and toasted in a skillet.  I didn't bother thawing the corn - just tossed the frozen kernels with the dry ingredients before combining with the wet.  Switched out the coriander for a couple shakes of nutmeg.  Don't worry about filling those muffin cups, they bake beautifully.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Brown sugar? Sure, if you prefer cake over corn bread.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
I made these vegan, so there were some substitutions made:
Aquafaba 
Soy milk with apple cider vinegar 

I also used Eastern European fine polenta. 
Doubled the recipe and grateful that I did. They are delicious like a cupcake. Maybe add frosting or cut the sugar. 

Note: I freshly ground my coriander, but could not taste it like other commentators mentioned.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
What is aquafaba and what is the ratio of the two substitutes?
Lactose intolerant

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Susan, aquafaba is the liquid from a can of chickpeas. It whips up well and is very versatile in many vegan recipes.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Next time, I might try cutting the sugar to 2 tbs. Also used only buttermilk, 1/2 cup. Delicious and simple.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I don’t care for corn kernels in my cornbread, so I skipped that part and the muffins were still delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Looks more like cake than bread. This is the best: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/3544-the-best-corn-bread

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I cut the sugar by a third, and the muffins were still plenty sweet. I used 1 percent milk because that’s what I had, and they were fine. A keeper.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Maybe because I’m at 5k feet, these spread, entirely into a sheet of cookies. Next time I’ll try chilling overnight. They smell amazing, though, so here’s hoping the second attempt is better.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: No sugar please!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I would double the amount to make a full pan.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: So easy and tasty

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: These are easy to throw together, which is nice. For me they were ok, the flavor didn’t particularly stand out to me so I will probably tweak the recipe or choose another next time.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Kate agree, I’ve had much better tasting corn muffins and wasn’t a fan of this recipe

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I've had trouble finding a cornbread recipe that I really like. They are often dry with no corn flavor. This was much better. I like cornbread with a pot of greens and this does the trick. I also like that it was made to be muffins which are easier to dip in the liquid of mixed greens.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Has anyone tried this without brown sugar.?I’m a Black Souther purist and don’t understand sugar in corn bread.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Can polenta be used in place of cornmeal?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Put it in an 8x8. Ended up very dense

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I found these too dense, not a good corn muffin texture, and not nearly enough corn meal taste

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
Interesting but missing dimension.  Love the coriander though! 
Am thinking some Parmesan cheese or something similar would help.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Loved these muffins except that the coriander got lost. Couldn’t really tell it was there. Thoughts?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: As a general rule, whenever Indian spices are mentioned I literally double them. Give it a try.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I found these muffins to be lacking good flavor and texture. They were sort of heavy and didn’t crisp up at all. I really wanted to like them but couldn’t.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I found them insufficiently tasty.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: While corn muffins “freeze beautifully” they are quite difficult to teheat without drying out or tasting stale. I recommend cubing the leftovers & freezing for dressing/stuffing, or (my favorite) frying in butter with safe for delicious croutons for soup or salad.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Switch out the buttermilk for yogurt?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Love the coriander in these cornbread muffins. I can imagine their being great still with less sugar. I used King Arthur Golden Wheat Flour for the flour and they came out fantastic.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: First toast the raw cornmeal in a dry skillet until fragrant. It will substantially amp the corn flavor.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Line cast iron muffin tins with bacon grease and you will cry from the perfection... Trust me.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: We lived in east Africa for many years and a staple on many tables was a condiment called pilipili hoho, used to ramp up the heat in any dish. Instead of using oil, it was common that the chilis and aromatics were steeped in either sherry, or more potently vodka. Occasionally vinegar was used in place of alcohol for a Tabasco-sauce taste. Any version adds a piquant kick to your dishes, highly recommended.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @Miriam: Apparently several cuisines add chilies to vodka - notably in Thailand. In the 1966 movie "You Only Live Twice", filmed almost entirely in Japan, Sean Connery's James Bond, having knocked out a hulking thug after an extended fight, opens an office cabinet. finds a bottle of spirits and, badly in need of refreshment, takes a swig. Immediately grimacing in distaste and pain, he glances at the label and discovers that he has just swallowed Siamese vodka.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Should we cut the Thai chiles or leave it whole. If left whole, how hot will the oil get? I suspect it will be milder than when cut into small pieces.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Try replacing Thai chiles with Panamanian or Scotchbonnet chiles.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Low heat.  It never sizzled.  Anyone know about how long to simmer it?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: just so complicated and so many dishes at the end.  9 steps should have been a clue.  really tasty and interesting but man-oh-man this was a chore

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed making this dish! I thought the mushroom “sauce” was a bit overpowering (I would probably just cook them in some butter and salt next time), but really enjoyed the chard two ways and thought the mix of the three items was tasty!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: The title was tantalizing but the multi-steps and hard to find ingredients (Oat milk cream..?) made an Ottolenghi recipe look like the Joy of Cooking. It would be nice to order this at a restaurant. For now, I will take the title as inspiration to make a polenta dish with crispy sautéed mushrooms and Swiss chard.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
I really wanted to like this, but somehow the whole was less than the sum of its parts. 

I liked the cooking method for the chard leaves and will likely use that again; likewise the chard stem pickles were fun. But despite the lovely fragrance of the mushroom sauce once it (finally) came together in my blender, I agree with another writer that a straight sauté (perhaps with chopped garlic) would yield a more satisfying result.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Left out jalapeños as I can’t eat them and subbed cream and butter for oat milk cream and oil in grits. Very disappointing. A lot of work and not much flavor. Added green Tabasco and a lot of cheese. The pickled chard stems ended up in the trash. Don’t waste your time and money.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: This really is complicated,yes!  I did it in two days.  Made the grits (polenta actually) and the pickles and prepped the chard on the first day. Finished the next evening. Enjoyed it somewhat. Saved the rest. After sitting in the fridge for a day everything improved. Now I’m really impressed. The pickles are a genius touch. Thanks!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: This is a whole lot of effort to cook and cleanup for a result that doesn't really feel worth it. If you are going to make this, season and taste as you go.  I ended up needing much more salt pepper than was called for. Generally speaking, this recipe was "ok", but I feel like I would rather have avoided the sauce altogether, and sautéed that onion and jalapeño to include with the Swiss Chard, and just browned the mushrooms with some olive oil and salt, and used white wine vinegar instead.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Fun mix of flavors and textures. I would advise less salt in the grits, however. I also limited my jalapeños to only including in the sauce and it was plenty spicy. All in all, fun and interesting! I’m glad I made it.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Obsessed with this recipe. I am eating the leftovers now & had to make a review - I love the Swiss chard both ways - with the mushrooms & sauce - so good. Didn’t have cilantro on hand so I used parsley instead! A whole jalapeño is rather much for the sauce to me. Love it will make again many times!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve found once I’ve cooked this through according to the recipe, it becomes much simpler to manage. Especially if it’s be has a habit of preparing pickled onions and peppers for other weekly dishes. I used whole milk and butter rather than the coconut cream, although I think the coconut would be an improvement, cow’s milk was all I had on hand.

Substitute mindfully and this is a great veggie dish!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I loved this recipe. I used quick grits that only take 5 minutes to make. The pickled and spice flavor go super well together. The creamy grits really serves this dish nicely. Would definitely make again.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Super delicious! I will definitely be making this again - it would be awesome with collards or kale. I didn’t have a lemon, so I subbed a splash of white vinegar and it turned out great. Served with chicken breasts.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I loved this… think it is one of the most interesting recipes you have. It was a lot of pieces, and I think next time I will make the sauce ahead, and prep the chard and make the pickle ahead. (I also do t like spicy so left that out.) then, this is a manageable recipe to make for company. It’s definitely worth making!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: While the dish was good taste wise, I have to throw my hat in with the too many steps, too many dishes. I don't feel that the result was worth the effort, thus my DNDO (Do Not Do Over) rating of this dish.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this recipe with a few modifications. I did not marinate the mushrooms in the sauce. I love the flavor of mistakes too much to cover them up! It was pretty spicy, even with half the seeds removed. I also blended in avocado to make it creamier. It was delicious served on top.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Use low sodium chicken broth to cook the grits.   Add cream (and cheese!) after the grits are tender.    The recipe for the chard is good but too much work and dirty dishes for the end result.   I saute the tough chard stems for several minutes to get them tender before I add the chard greens to the cast iron skillet, cover and simmer until done.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
Closely followed this recipe. 1.5 hr. prep time is accurate. It isn't a difficult recipe & a pleasant way to relax after a workday.
The jalapenos offset the bland grits. The grits were a perfect consistency. (Per the tip, I soaked stone-ground grits [6 hours] & they only took 30 min to cook after coming to a boil. Since the grits were already soaking in the measured amt of water I continually whisked them while bringing them to a boil to prevent lumping.
Glad I didn't add salt while cook'g grits

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I should have read the comments before embarking on this journey. This recipe was very fussy and complicated, but each element lacked flavor, with the exception of the pickled chard stems. The worst part is the mushroom sauce, which is actually delicious when it comes out of the food processor, but imparts nothing to the mushrooms as a marinade, and loses all flavor when it hits the cast iron skillet. This is the first times NYT Cooking has failed me, but this was a huge disappointment.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
If you put the grits in the water before heating, it's less likely to clump. 

But I wouldn't worry about some sticking to the bottom of the pan. It does no harm and is probably impossible to prevent anyway, not without constant stirring.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Way too much work for the result and the salt ratio is way off on the grits. This was inedible and went in the trash after cleaning all the dishes. I love NYT recipes but this was my worst experience by far. Sometimes you think 'I could make this better with a tweak.' No.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was pretty good. 
I didn't think the sauce for the mushrooms was very spicy, but maybe I just got a mild jalapeno. 
I only had 1 jalapeno so I subbed 1 tsp crushed red pepper when I prepared the chard stems. 
Downside: I did find this recipe to be a bit time consuming.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: has this been made with more traditional products - like milk or cream or half-and-half? Unsweetened oat milk cream (along with its recommended substitutions) are new to me.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Yes, dairy cream or half and half work fine; your goal is just to make the grits creamier.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Is grit oat or polenta?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Grits are corn. Polenta is corn. They are cousins but can be used interchangeably.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: quinoa for gluten intolerant... i agree about the number of steps and pots! my italian k i s s method, evoo, garlic, mushrooms and then, as they release their yummy juices, chard (or spinach). two pots/pans is simple and quick ~ all in the time it takes for the quinoa to cook!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Grits and polenta are also gluten-free, so no need to sub quinoa unless you prefer its taste.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Not bad but too much effort for a mixed result. Instructions are confusing. We won't make this one again.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I followed recipe exactly and thought it was excellent!  My carnivorous husband really like it and wants me to make it again soon!  It wasn't difficult and was definitely worth the effort.  There wasn't much left over, about 1 small serving. Packaged each part separately.  Reheated well in microwave, each part in their separate containers; the pickled stems taken from fridge early enough to sit out and be roughly room temp (not microwaved).

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I liked the inventiveness of the pickled chard stems but will leave them out if I make this again.  The mushrooms and the chard leaves are lovely, though.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
As others have noted, this was a LOT of steps for unremarkable results. Suggestions:
1. Instant grits - as noted by another review, a real time-saver
2. Maybe a substitute the cream in the grits for goat cheese and little thyme to amp up the flavor
3. Chard leaves would be better massaged with a very small amount of oil and roasted in the oven to make them crispy and give the dish texture
4. Mushroom - roast or sauté until brown then toss with sauce
5. Sauce - amp up flavor significantly

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I agree with others that this was tasty, but an ordeal to make. Took much longer than 1 1/2 hours. The sauce for the mushrooms was delicious and I hope to make it to augment other recipes.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: I'll probably try with coolapeno, which is my go-to non-spicy substitute for jalapeno. I grow them and freeze the abundance to have on hand.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe that inspires the following modifications: I used polenta instead of grits and added some feta and olive tapenade to the chard then gave it a  quick saute just before serving.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Don’t get me wrong the flavor composition was great and delectable. But, the recipe was overly convoluted. Who has the time or patience for such a needlessly complicated process? The end result was hardly justifying the effort put in. Any chef could achieve same result quicker and more easily by not following the prescribed method. Overall, a disappointing experience that I wouldn't recommend to anyone looking for a hassle-free meal.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: You lost me when you asked for chopped cilantro to go into a food processor. That’s just bad recipe writing. That said, it tastes really good. So, there’s that.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody:
I cooked 1 cup of polenta in the pressure cooker with 4 cups of water for 20 minutes, then added some olive oil, butter, Parmesan and a bit of oat milk.  Added probably 2 tsp of salt at the end to taste.

I sautéed the chard stems and shiitake mushrooms, then deglazed with 1/4 cup Marsala.  I added the chard leaves with some chicken stock and salt, and sautéed all that until the chard was tender. Added a little Aleppo pepper and a splash of sherry vinegar at the end.  Served on polenta with some more Parmesan on top.  Quite good, my wife said she could eat it three times a week!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Was a little bit nervous about this one based on the other comments (which I hadn’t read until after I bought ingredients, fortunately as it turns out), but while this DID result in a lot of dirty pans, I thought with some modifications this worked well and was worth the time investment. I left the chard stems and mushrooms to sit in the vinegar and marinade respectively much longer than the recipe recommended (1 hr ) and put extra garlic in the marinade for the mushrooms. I also made the grits significantly less heart healthy - used regular cream, swapped the olive oil for 2 tbsp butter, and added cheese to taste (I used jack cheese). The only complaint I got from the audience was that there wasn’t enough! (This was just barely enough for 3 people, if making for more than that, or for 3 hungry people, I recommend doubling the recipe.)

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Creamy Grits With Mushrooms and Chard Recipe
commentBody: Time & labor intensive, but well suited for a holiday gathering for vegan and non-vegan guests alike. Plates beautifully for a special occasion buffet. Served with Miso Marinated Pork Roast for the carnivores. A definite hit.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: My mother, from Curacao, would put leftover plain fungi in greased bowl, refrigerate overnight.  Fungi becomes solid, unmold, slice, fry in butter, and serve with syrup for breakfast the next morning!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: In Barbados this is called coo coo

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: People are calling this grits . It's not. Grits are typically made from more coarsely-ground cornmeal.  This uses fine cornmeal and requires okra as an ingredient.  Both of those things make this taste and feel very different from standard southern grits.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Thanks George. This isn't grits at all. It's not polenta either.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Not quite George...southern grits are made from dried hominy.  Which is corn dried with lye....very different than cornmeal.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: @geoffi think you mean hominy grits ( treated with lye)  which are not the same as “ plain” grits  which  are not treated

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: It's grits, first cooked by Native Americans, who taught the British to make it.   The British called it grits.  Italians called it polenta.  You can add virtually anything to it - grits and shrimp, grits and vegetables, grits with ham and cheese, etc.  It is a staple of southern US food and so simple to make that I'm surprised young people from the Islands don't know how to cook it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Grits is coarsely ground corn, compared to finely ground cornmeal.  We get our grits ground fresh from the Logan Turnpike Mill in Georgia.  They take 30 minutes to cook and soften.  The recipe here specifies  cornmeal and says it will get thick very quickly, so it doesn’t sound as if you could substitute grits, unless they are the awful tasting quick grits from a supermarket box.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Sorry, Mary. This isn't grits. Neither is it polenta. The corn is processed differently. #Tennessean who grew up on grits and loves polenta.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Most useful tip to prevent lumpy polenta- like dishes:  Stir only clockwise ( or anti - clockwise ). Do not change direction!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
First introduced to fungi honeymooning on St John 31 yrs ago and was my favorite part of any meal.  This was pre google and could never find a recipe.  Forgot about it. 
Enjoying the banter about grits , not grits.
Eastern Europeans do the same with borscht and other dishes.  We all have our versions of whatever, and what we grew up with, of course, is best. We like what we like.  Looking forward to enjoying fungi again.  Thanks for sharing.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Where is the fungus in the fungi?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: And how is it pronounced? "Fun Guy"? "Fun Ghee"? "Fun Jee"?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody:
@David Jackson

I watched some videos online about this. They pronounced it “fun-chee,” but it goes by many names. One cook said that she and her family don’t use okra because nobody in her family likes it. Different cooks added different aromatics and cooked it to different textures.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Too much salt. Next time only putting in one tsp.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I've had this with smashed avocado mixed in instead of okra a real treat

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is easy to make and a delicious side for fish or chicken. Also followed the advice below and fried leftovers for breakfast with an egg on top. Super. Have been to Petite Pump Room many times waiting for the ferry to Tortola, but never had the time to have more than a sandwich. Happy to see the restaurant owners and Chef Jackson get some publicity!

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Try it with masa.  The lye treatment of corn modifies it in such a way that your body can utilize the niacin in the corn.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: It's cornmeal mush w/okra.  Mine: 2 C water, 2 C milk, 1 C cornmeal, 1.5 tsp kosher salt.  When cornmeal has thickened, add 2 beaten eggs, 1 c shredded sharp cheese tempered with some cooked cornmeal.  Stir and bake until eggs and cheese are "absorbed".  Add diced Mexican squash (looks like zucchini but is firmer and tastes better) at the last 5 minutes to retain some firmness.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: This is just grits, first cooked by Native Americans who taught the British to make it.   It is a staple porridge in the American South and is called polenta by Italians.   Shrimp and grits, cheesy grits, grits and eggs, grits with ham, grits and blackeyed peas, etc.  Adding okra is a nice touch and probably the only African contribution to the dish.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: What’s the difference between this and grits and polenta?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Sliced, boiled Okra. Grits and polenta don’t have Okra. If you mean what’s the difference between grits, polenta and yellow cornmeal, it can be the grind and/or what color corn, white, yellow, blue, was used to make it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Traditionally grits were ground from corn hominy, corn that had been soaked in wood-ash lye to dissolve the seed coat and soften the kernel. The kernel can be soaked loose and removed if you wanted a beautiful preparation, but that removes a lot of nutrition too. Polenta and cornmeal are not treated before grinding. Today some recipes, and even my bag of Bob's Red Mill grits, pretend there is no difference between them.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I meant that the germ, which is often dark colored, could be removed from the kernel.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: What's the difference between this and polenta other than the okra? What does okra add to the dish other than it's sliminess?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Okra "slimyness" is just liquid fiber and helps the coucou set up more firmly - also adding a bit of taste.  Coucou itself is quite bland but is a terrific vehicle for Creole sauce (as in steamed flying fish).

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: If okra get slimy you usually overcooked them. I don't know if this dish requires it here, though.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Is the cornmeal served barely cooked? I thought it took more than a few minutes for cornmeal to cook  and lose its grittiness.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Would this work with millet instead of corn?

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: What are you supposed to do with the okra? Step 2 says to cook it, then it isn't mentioned again.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Add 1/2 of the cornmeal to the same water as the okra very very slowly to reduce lumpiness.  Then on to step 3.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: Cut butter to 1 1/2 tbsp.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Also, one can purchase tomato sauce with no salt added. Or make your own. And add seasonings that are not blended with salt. You can do it!  It's the heart of the recipe here that counts.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Actually I think recipe substitutions are illegal

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious, though I doubled the amount of Creole seasoning for a little extra kick. But 30 minutes is an extremely optimistic projection. Just prepping the onions, peppers, and garlic took 20 minutes, and the sauce took a good 45 minutes. Worth every minute.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: see Shrimp Creole by Vallery Lomas in NYTC for homemade Creole seasoning

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I’m from St. Thomas and there nothing quite as delicious as fish and fungi! Too bad they didn’t include the  fungi part but it’s not too difficult to make. Just think polenta.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: for anyone who missed it, the headnotes include a link to a fungi recipe: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022093-fungi

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Substitutions for red snapper: Grouper. Sea bass. Tilapia. Cod. Catfish. Queen snapper. Haddock [] Takes at least an hour to make. [] Sauce freezes well and is good on eggs.[] Suggested to make fungi (similar to polenta) [] FOR THE CREOLE SEASONING (OPTIONAL):
1 tablespoon smoked paprika
1 tablespoon chili powder
1 teaspoon onion powder
1 teaspoon garlic powder
½ teaspoon cayenne pepper (or less, if desired)
½ teaspoon fine sea salt
½ teaspoon black pepper
[]

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I had no Lawry's or Creole seasoning so made my own with fresh basil, oregano, thyme and a host of other ingredients. Very easy.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Nice work but sounds like you made a type of Italian seasoning, instead of Creole. 🤭. I'm sure it was lovely.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This also made a huge amount of sauce, I plan to freeze the extra.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  reminded us of the snapper you can find in the LoLo's in St. Martin.  The recipe makes a ton of sauce.  Luckily it freezes and well and is equally good on eggs.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This dish transported me straight to the Caribbean! Excellent all around. Special enough to make for guests or eat on a weeknight. Loved it!!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I was so happy to see this recipe, as I am a native of the Virgin Islands. Will give it a try

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I used baked sole because it looked good at the store.  The sauce was lovely and very versatile

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
There should be some celery in there too....

I was taught a trick to put a small sachet of pickling spice in the sauce too.....you might like it!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is really good. The most time consuming part of the recipe is chopping the vegetables but no big deal. Probably 20 minutes tops.  It’s totally worth it!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: what is creole seasoning? can we make it instead of Lawry's

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
RE: Creole seasoning recipe
africanbites.com/creole-seasoning/
There are other recipes with added salt; I prefer this one because I like to add salt to taste.
You can possibly simplify the recipe by using one kind of pepper (I don't keep white pepper around) and one kind of chili pepper based on your heat preference.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was super good!! Love the flavors with one of my favorite fish. So, I tried to follow closely - here are some things I did differently due to access to ingredients. I pureed diced tomatoes to make "sauce". I used rice vinegar. Made my own Creole seasoning from Vallery Lomas. The result was delicious!! The only thing I'll change next time is to reduce the water added. I felt it wasn't as thick as I'd like.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent and fairly easy recipe as written. Just be realistic about how long it takes to sweat aromatics to "starting to brown": This takes at least 20 minutes at medium heat. I covered the pan for 5-10 minutes to jump start the sweating and the removed the lid to evaporate the liquid for like another 10 min so that the browning can begin.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Fried Snapper With Creole Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I got some fresh snapper from the farmer’s market and used local seasoning mix similar to lawrys (had habanero too). It came out incredible! Also made some rice, couldn’t resist. Worth it!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Here's what I do to prevent losing the cornmeal crust while shallow frying catfish:  first, scrape off excess buttermilk marinade; second,  quickly dip fish in seasoned flour and shake off excess; third, dip fish in beaten egg thinned with a little water; fourth, dip into cornmeal; and, finally, chill fish pieces for 15-30 minutes.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is very close to Julia Child’s approach to breading. She suggests a bit of olive oil in the egg mixture will help the coating adhere. And she says to put whatever you are coating on a rack for at least 15 minutes to let the coating dry a bit. Has never failed me.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Batter won't pull away from fish and/or get stuck on frying basket if you let it 'swim' for a moment.  Lift breaded portion with tongs, holding on to tongs - immerse the portion in hot oil and swish back & forth gently for about 10-15 seconds.  Then, let go and it'll fry up beautifully.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: The new frypans I recently bought are amazingly less sticky than my old ones. I don't know whether this is due to a more advanced non-stick coating, or just because the teflon on the old ones got more sticky with use. The advantage is not just in no longer needing your hold-up method, but also in much reduced oil use and cleaning effort.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: On the East Coast anyway, you can get fresh or frozen fresh-caught catfish. Because catfish are devouring blue crabs in the Chesapeake bay they are being caught and offered at market. Way superior to farmed if you’ve tasted the real item, say out of the Mississippi.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Really good catfish recipe.  Soaking the pieces in the buttermilk mixture is an absolute necessity.  The longer the better the taste is going to be.  Had some leftovers and used then for fish tacos. Definitely a keeper. Thanks

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Here on Charleston's coast, the local fish fry favorite is whiting.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, except no onion powder (ran out), and only 2 tsp salt.  still too salty, will decrease next time.  Shallow fried in cast iron, crust was perfect, no problems sticking to the pan.  Will cook again.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I don't like to deep fry so I tried shallow frying this. The batter tended to pull away from the fish and stuck to the bottom of the pan. I scraped it up. It all tasted good, but we had a platter of mostly naked fish with crispy sheets of batter. I'd be interested to hear if anyone shallow fried this with success.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made this a while back, and I agree the quantity of salt is too much, and so are the buttermilk and cornmeal measurements! I'll try it with half as much next time. After breading the fish, I used the buttermilk and corn meal in a batch of cornbread- SOOOOO Good! I worried it might be fishy, but it was just amazing, and a nice way to reduce our food waste. I didn't measure the oil, just poured it about an inch deep, and used it for savory baking and salad dressings afterward, as well.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Thank you. The left-over half of the cornmeal and buttermilk was just the right amount to follow your suggestion and make this: https://cafedelites.com/easy-buttermilk-cornbread/ The hot sauce in the buttermilk (I used Frank's) actually gave the cornbread a pleasant tang.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Be sure to buy U.S. Farm- raised catfish (unless you catch it yourself!) Simmons Farms is a reliable company.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I don't like to deep fry -- could this be shallow fried/sauteed?  (If not, I'll have my fried fish in a restaurant...)

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Don’t be shy on the seasoning quantities in the buttermilk and cornflower mixtures, a lot is lost in the fry. I also added mustard powder and smoked paprika powder. Hold submerged in oil for a few seconds before releasing into oil, to ensure it does not stick to bottom/lose batter. Serve with favourite hot sauce and enjoy!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: These reheated excellently from the refrigerator the following day in a cast iron skillet @300° for 20 minutes, and were served as po’boys, with tartar sauce, lettuce and tomato.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: For those who are dairy free, we used oat milk instead of buttermilk and it was still delicious!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: In reference to Charlie's comment a year ago;  I filter used oil through a coffee filter fitted into a small sieve.  It filters through slowly so I just add a little oil to the sieve whenever I pass by.  The filtered oil can be used at least one more time and we usually use it several more times.  However this would not be a good idea if you burned the oil.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: A good substitute if you live near a northeastern lake is walleye. Haven't had good store bought catfish in ages.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Generally I bake catfish, so I wonder if it would work here. My recipe has hot sauce and beer rather than buttermilk, than rolled in cornmeal, so it's not too different.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Thanks

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Old, no longer useable oil can be poured into cat litter (new or used) which will absorb it and which can then be discarded in the trash.  The people who pick up my trash don't like picking up liquids that can splash back at them and make a mess in the truck.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: We had to get a little creative because we couldn't find Catfish or plain cornmeal at our local grocery store. Instead we made it with cod and a 50/50 mix we found that had cornmeal and corn flour. It actually turned out really well!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was a fantastic and easy meal. I coupled it with an heirloom tomato and basil salad with a vinaigrette and it balanced dinner out very nicely.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: One should mix in AP flour to the corn meal as corn meal burns easily and tends to crumble.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I first ate fried catfish in New Orleans, but served in a po' boy sandwich. I have cooked this many times, pretty much as described in the recipe, but sauted instead of deep-fried and then squirted over with lemon juice.  My sandwich is made on a crusty French or Italian long roll smeared with a little hot-sauced mayo, and then  filled with some lettuce, topped with the fried fish, a couple of tomato slices  and a 1 or 2  thin lemon sllces. You need to open wide to eat - Yum!

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made in my air fryer. 400 for 12 min, turning it 6 min. Used some old bay in the cornmeal. Was great

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: What do we do with all the leftover oil?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It can be used several times for fish, nothing else. When it becomes dark or off smelling, you can pour it in a plastic bottle and throw it in the trash. Many restaurants recycle their oil so if you are friendly with one, they may take it off your hands. Do not pour it down the sink or toilet.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I keep an empty gallon container and fill it after the old oil has cooled. I don't fry too often, so the container has become a part of the family. When it finally gets full, I'll drive north and put it on a raft, set it on fire and give it a proper send-off. If it doesn't get full, I'll have something to leave my daughter. And then she can play Viking.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I never reuse the oil!  This recipe is too good to ruin it with reused oil. When cool I poor it back into the store bought bottle and discard it.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: My Papa always reused oil for fish frying. He said the secret is when you reheat it for the next batch to first fry an onion cut in half. It takes out the old taste. It's always worked for me lol

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I POUR it...

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Anybody try it with an air fryer?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I did this. It worked fairly well, though it took several batches to finish all pieces. There's enough moisture and fat in the fish and the marinade that it didn't need additional basting with oil.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Could I use something like grouper or snapper? Probably more expensive than catfish, but we live in Florida, so...

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I cook the catfish as described but use it as part of a catfish Poor Boy sandwich that I first ate in New Orleans.  It is served all over the city with slight variations depending on the cook...A catfish fillet cooked as above layered over lettuce and tomato slices, and topped with very thin slices of lemon in a crisp French roll. Hot sauce to taste. Delicious.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Excellent procedure and results.  Crust stays on the catfish and remains crispy/crunchy--not forever but for several (20?) minutes.  Spicy marinade works well--and simplifies the cornmeal mixture, a virtue.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Don’t add so much salt to the corn meal for dredging.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I used only two cups of oil and fried the catfish in a skillet. Two cups was plenty and I think four would have been excessive, especially for my small kitchen and stove. The oil didn't cover the whole piece of fish, but turning each piece over took care of frying them evenly on both sides. I made sure the oil was really hot. It worked well and turned out delicious. This is a great recipe.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Can someone recommend a non-dairy substitute for the buttermilk marinade?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this great recipe using an air fryer yet?  If so, what was the cooking times and any noted variations?

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a fan of catfish, but will use the buttermilk mixture and cornmeal for other types of fish. Would this work with chicken???

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Too salty!!! I added all that salt to the marinade thinking it would not considerably permeate the fish, and just flavor it. 
No. Too much salt. 
And the corn meal crust had no salt in it. 
I’d say, take the salt content down by at least a tsp.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I first ate fried catfish in New Orleans, served in a poorboy sandwich.  Returning home I made my own version, based on what was served throughout the French Quarter: Fried catfish whole fillet, cooked as in this recipe, with cornmeal and hot sauce in a crusty roll on a lettuce base, with chopped scallions. a squirt of lemon juice on top or else a couple of very thin slices of lemon.  I don't marinate the catfish or deep-fry. I  find that just enough oil to coat the pan well works for me.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made according to the recipe, deep fried outside in peanut oil at 350f on a propane camp stove. Delicious, crispy, succulent. Leftovers destined for tacos.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I prefer to shallow fry the fish and modified another cook’s suggestion for getting the coating to stay on. First swipe off some of the marinade, dip in flour, dip back in marinade (instead of egg mixture in other suggestion) and then into cornmeal. Saves mixing up the egg and does the binding job just as well (even tastier). I do use whole fillets because I like them in sandwiches, so that makes the job a lot less fussy. If need to cut in half, do it just before frying.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
So good!
- Reduced salt to 1.5 t and this was just right
- Had 2 lb blue catfish
- After marinating, had 1.5 c buttermilk and 1/4 c cornmeal > added this to hushpuppy batter :)

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Mississippi farm raised catfish is the absolute best kind to get and tastes totally different than wild caught catfish. Farm raised catfish are fed puffs that float on the water which prevents them from getting the muddy taste that most catfish get because they’re bottom feeders.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Deep fried at 350* for 4:30. Let coating sit on for 15 mins. and swim each piece in the oil before letting go.

author: Korsha Wilson
recipeTitle: Todd Richards’s Fried Catfish With Hot Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Made this for lunch today with 6 oz swai fillets, cut in half crosswise.  Rested 15 minutes, not five, on a wire rack over rimmed baking sheet, to fully hydrate the coating.  Cooked fully in six minutes.   Prefectly crunchy, delicious!  Served with NYT's only hush puppy recipe (also a winner), cole slaw, homegrown tomatoes, iced tea and strawberry poke cake for dessert.  No supper needed. What a treat!
